solver_view.stem: A Li-ion cell is heated uniformly in an oven held at 150°C. No other data are available. Which statements are justified solely from this information and general physical reasoning?
options: A SEI decomposition exotherm can be thermally accessible during the test.
B The cell will inevitably enter thermal runaway because 150°C exceeds 120°C.
C SEI may fail to prevent anode–electrolyte contact, enabling exothermic reaction of intercalated lithium with electrolyte.
D The dominant hazard must be internal short-circuit rather than chemical heat release.
E The gas generation rate can be inferred quantitatively from the oven setpoint alone.
F If the cell survives 150°C, it is guaranteed safe under all lower-temperature duties.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 150°C exceeds the cited SEI exothermic decomposition range, so the pathway is accessible.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Exceeding a threshold for possible reactions does not prove inevitable runaway without kinetics/heat loss data.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Above 120°C, SEI may not protect; lithium–electrolyte exotherm can occur.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No information indicates a short-circuit event; chemical exotherms are explicitly plausible at this temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A setpoint alone cannot quantify gas generation without reaction rates, state-of-charge, and venting specifics.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Passing one condition does not guarantee safety across all lower-temperature duty cycles or abuse modes.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish plausible pathways from inevitability claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Temperature enables reactions but not guaranteed outcomes
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing runaway from setpoint alone
Inferring dominant failure mode without signals
Generalizing pass/fail to all conditions

solver_view.stem: A lab performs two abuses on the same cell type: (i) external short-circuit using <5 mΩ across terminals, (ii) nail forced through the cell at 8 cm/s. Which statements are justified from these descriptions alone?
options: A Test (i) is designed to drive high current by minimizing external resistance.
B Test (ii) can physically breach internal layers, potentially bypassing normal electrochemical pathways.
C Test (i) guarantees SEI decomposition because current must heat the anode above 90–120°C.
D Test (ii) guarantees thermal runaway because penetration necessarily triggers an internal short.
E From the given rates/resistances, the total gas yield can be calculated without additional information.
F Test (ii) is less severe than (i) for every chemistry and SOC because it is mechanical, not electrical.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A very low external resistance across terminals is explicitly a short-circuit setup for high current.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Forcing a nail through a cell at a prescribed rate is a mechanical intrusion that can breach internal layers.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: High current is possible, but temperature rise to SEI decomposition is not guaranteed without thermal details.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Penetration may or may not create an internal short; runaway is not guaranteed from description alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas yield depends on internal chemistry, temperature history, and venting; not derivable from rate/resistance alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports universal severity ordering across chemistries/SOC; both can trigger exothermic pathways.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identify what test descriptions do and don’t imply
analysis.primary_boundary: Setup parameters don’t determine outcomes or severity ranking
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing temperature thresholds from short-circuit setup
Guaranteeing internal short/runaway from nail test
Universal severity ranking across chemistries/SOC

solver_view.stem: An engineer proposes a safety argument: “Our cell passed an oven exposure at 150°C, therefore the SEI must remain protective, and lithium–electrolyte reactions cannot occur in service.” Which critique(s) are justified?
options: A The argument conflicts with the possibility that SEI protection can fail above 120°C.
B Passing one oven condition does not exclude exothermic SEI decomposition pathways existing at 90–120°C.
C The argument is valid because 150°C is the consumer standard oven temperature, so it bounds all service conditions.
D Even if no runaway occurred, it does not prove lithium–electrolyte reaction cannot occur; it may be limited by heat removal/extent.
E The argument is invalid only if an external short-circuit (<5 mΩ) was also applied during the oven test.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SEI may not protect above 120°C, contradicting “must remain protective”.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI decomposition can occur at 90–120°C; passing 150°C doesn’t negate existence of that pathway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A standard test temperature does not bound all service conditions or mechanisms; that conclusion overreaches.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Non-occurrence of runaway doesn’t prove reactions cannot occur; reaction extent may be insufficient to escalate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Critique does not depend on adding a short-circuit; the SEI-related claims are independent of that condition.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Evaluate safety claims for logical overreach
analysis.primary_boundary: Pass/fail doesn’t prove mechanisms absent
disallowed_extrapolations: Standards temperature implies universal safety
No runaway implies no exothermic reactions
Adding unstated simultaneous abuses

solver_view.prompt: You are asked to write one paragraph for a hazard review. The only known facts are: an oven exposure uses 150°C, SEI can decompose exothermically at 90–120°C, and above 120°C SEI may fail to protect the anode, enabling exothermic lithium–electrolyte reaction. State what you can and cannot legitimately conclude about runaway inevitability and about whether these reactions can occur.

ground_truth.gold_answer: At 150°C, temperature is within the range where SEI decomposition is thermally accessible and above the regime where SEI may cease protecting the anode, so exothermic SEI decomposition and lithium–electrolyte reaction are plausible hazards. However, from temperature alone you cannot conclude thermal runaway is inevitable, nor can you conclude the SEI must remain protective or that these reactions cannot occur; their extent depends on conditions not provided.
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing runaway from temperature alone
Claiming reactions cannot occur if no runaway
Generalizing to all service/duty conditions

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Write bounded conclusions from minimal thermal evidence
analysis.primary_boundary: Plausibility vs inevitability under incomplete information

solver_view.stem: A Li-ion cell is cycled under normal operation. Later, a separate abuse event drives it into thermal runaway. Select statements that are justified about gas generation mechanisms/trends across these two regimes.
options: A Gas can be generated during normal operation via electrolyte redox at highly delithiated cathode or fully lithiated anode, especially during formation cycles.
B Under normal operation, gas generation is negligible; meaningful gas appears only during thermal runaway.
C During thermal runaway, gas production is strongly enhanced and may include flammable species such as O2, C2H4, and C2H6.
D The presence of O2 in thermal runaway implies the electrolyte itself is the only oxygen source.
E If thermal runaway occurs, earlier formation-cycle gas generation must have been the root cause.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Normal operation can produce gas from electrolyte oxidation/reduction at highly delithiated cathode or fully lithiated anode, especially in formation cycles.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Normal operation gas generation is explicitly described; not restricted to thermal runaway only.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Thermal runaway enhances gas production and includes flammable gases like O2, C2H4 and C2H6.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: O2 presence does not uniquely identify electrolyte as sole oxygen source from the stated information.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal runaway triggers include several abuse modes; earlier formation gas is not shown necessary or causal.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Regime separation: normal vs runaway gas mechanisms
analysis.primary_boundary: No causal attribution from coexistence
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring unique oxygen source from O2 detection
Claiming formation gas is necessary for runaway

solver_view.stem: During an electrochemical test at 3.8 V, a cell shows increased CO2 and detectable O2. Which interpretations are justified given only the reaction information provided?
options: A CO2 and O2 can be produced by Li2CO3 decomposition at 3.8 V via 2Li2CO3 → 4Li+ + 4e− + 2CO2 + O2.
B Observing CO2 at 3.8 V proves thermal runaway is underway.
C The amount of CO2 can be predicted from voltage alone without knowing how much Li2CO3 is present.
D CO2 must originate from singlet oxygen reacting with EC, since that pathway yields CO2.
E O2 detection proves the cathode is releasing oxygen from its lattice.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A specific decomposition reaction at 3.8 V produces CO2 and O2 from Li2CO3, consistent with these observed gases.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CO2 at 3.8 V can arise from Li2CO3 decomposition; it is not a definitive indicator of thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Voltage alone does not fix reaction extent; gas amount depends on available Li2CO3 and reaction progress.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The EC pathway also produces CO and H2O; CO2 presence alone cannot uniquely identify that mechanism.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: O2 can be produced by Li2CO3 decomposition; the oxygen source cannot be uniquely attributed from O2 detection alone.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Avoid over-diagnosis from partial gas signals
analysis.primary_boundary: Gas detection is not unique to a mechanism
disallowed_extrapolations: Diagnosing thermal runaway from CO2 alone
Assigning oxygen source from O2 alone

solver_view.stem: A cell experiences an event where internal temperature rises rapidly. Select statements that are justified about triggers and possible gas chemistry outcomes.
options: A Overcharging, overheating, external short circuits, and internal short circuits can trigger thermal runaway via fast internal temperature rise.
B A fast internal temperature rise uniquely indicates an internal short circuit rather than overheating.
C Thermal runaway can enhance production of flammable gases, including O2, C2H4, and C2H6.
D If flammable gases are produced, thermal runaway must have been caused by overheating rather than overcharging.
E Thermal runaway requires the electrolyte to be oxidized only at the cathode surface, not at the anode.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Multiple abuse modes are listed as thermal runaway triggers and are associated with fast increase of internal temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Fast temperature rise is not unique; several triggers can cause it, including overheating and overcharging.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Gas production is strongly enhanced during thermal runaway and includes flammable gases such as O2, C2H4 and C2H6.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Flammable gas production under thermal runaway does not identify which trigger caused the runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Electrolyte redox and gas generation can occur at both highly delithiated cathode and fully lithiated anode surfaces.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Discriminate triggers vs symptoms and outcomes
analysis.primary_boundary: Triggers cannot be inferred from outcomes alone
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring specific trigger from gas species
Inferring short-circuit uniquely from fast temperature rise

solver_view.prompt: You measure CO2 and CO during an off-normal event and suspect a chemical pathway involving oxygen species reacting with electrolyte solvent. State one reaction consistent with generating CO2 and CO from ethylene carbonate, and explain one key limitation: what this observation alone cannot prove about the cell’s overall failure mode.

ground_truth.gold_answer: A consistent reaction is singlet oxygen reacting with ethylene carbonate: 2(1O2) + EC → 2CO2 + CO + H2O. Detecting CO2 and CO alone cannot prove thermal runaway or uniquely identify the oxygen source or trigger.
must_include: Cannot prove thermal runaway from gases alone
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming gases alone diagnose thermal runaway
Claiming specific trigger from gas composition
Claiming oxygen must be from cathode lattice

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Use reaction equations while respecting evidential limits
analysis.primary_boundary: Gas species do not uniquely establish failure mode

solver_view.stem: A stacked lithium-ion cell is fully penetrated by a metal nail such that every electrode layer is shorted by the nail. You instrument only the external connecting tabs and the nail, and you observe near-zero tab current during the event. Which conclusions are justified?
options: A Near-zero tab current can occur even with severe layer-to-layer shorts.
B Near-zero tab current implies no significant Joule heating occurs anywhere in the cell.
C Tab-current sensing alone is insufficient to rule out internal short-circuit during complete penetration.
D Near-zero tab current implies the nail is electrically insulated from the electrodes.
E Near-zero tab current proves the cell was not fully penetrated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Complete penetration can short each layer via the nail while no current flows through connecting tabs.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No-tab-current does not bound internal loop currents; heating cannot be excluded from tab data alone.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: If shorts close within stacked layers, tab current can be absent, so tabs can miss the fault.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Near-zero tab current is consistent with strong nail-electrode contact forming internal closed loops, not insulation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states fully penetrated cells can show no tab current; so this observation does not disprove full penetration.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interpretation limits of tab-current as ISC indicator
analysis.primary_boundary: Internal closed-loop shorts can bypass tabs entirely
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring internal heating magnitude from tab current alone

solver_view.stem: Two identical cells undergo nail penetration under otherwise similar conditions. In Test 1, the nail material is changed to have 10× lower electrical resistivity than in Test 2. You are told that, in nail penetration, contact resistance is usually several orders of magnitude larger than the nail’s internal resistance. Which statements are justified?
options: A Reducing nail resistivity by 10× will usually have little effect on total short-circuit resistance.
B Reducing nail resistivity by 10× will usually increase short-circuit current by about 10×.
C Engineering effort to reduce contact resistance could change total resistance more than changing nail material conductivity.
D Total resistance is dominated by nail internal resistance, so nail material choice is the primary lever.
E From these facts alone, you can guarantee the heating rate is unchanged between tests.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: If contact resistance dominates, changing a much smaller nail resistance tends not to shift total resistance much.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A 10× current rise assumes nail resistance dominates, which contradicts contact-resistance-dominated total resistance.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Because contact resistance dominates total resistance, reducing contact resistance can be a stronger lever than nail resistivity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The stem states the opposite: total resistance is dominated by contact resistance, not nail internal resistance.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Even if total resistance changes little, heating rate depends on current and distribution; it cannot be guaranteed unchanged.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Dominant-resistance reasoning and lever selection
analysis.primary_boundary: Contact resistance dominates; nail resistance often negligible
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing heating rate from resistance dominance alone

solver_view.stem: A 1.5 cm thick stacked cell is punctured at 8 cm/s. You must design a trigger to record the earliest internal fault onset, but you can only assume the provided puncture speed and typical complete puncture time for 1–2 cm thickness. Which trigger strategy is justified?
options: A Start high-rate data logging before puncture begins; full puncture can complete in under 1 s.
B It is safe to start logging 5 s after the puncture begins; complete puncture is slower than that.
C Sample tab current at 1 Hz; puncture dynamics are slow compared with 1 s.
D Use only post-test inspection; timing resolution is unnecessary because puncture always lasts minutes.
E Conclude puncture time is exactly thickness divided by speed; no uncertainty remains.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Typical speed and thickness imply complete puncture in less than 1 s in most cases, so logging must start early.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Contradicts the provided bound: complete puncture for 1–2 cm thickness is often under 1 s, not >5 s.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: If puncture completes in under 1 s, 1 Hz sampling can miss the transient onset entirely.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Provided timing is sub-second, so post-test-only cannot capture onset timing needed for early-fault recording.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The stem gives a typical speed and a typical time bound; it does not remove variability or guarantee exact kinematic timing.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Measurement strategy under sub-second failure timescales
analysis.primary_boundary: Typical puncture completion can be <1 s
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming exact puncture duration from typical speed alone

solver_view.prompt: You are investigating gas hazards during battery abuse. The only chemistry fact you may assume is: LiPF6 decomposes at 107 °C and generates HF. Write a conservative engineering judgment statement about what can and cannot be concluded regarding HF risk if a test report only says “the cell surface exceeded 110 °C” with no internal measurements. Keep the statement actionable but avoid unjustified certainty.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Because LiPF6 can decompose at 107 °C and generate HF, temperatures at or above this threshold imply HF generation is plausible if LiPF6 is present and actually reaches that temperature. However, a surface temperature above 110 °C alone does not prove the internal salt reached 107 °C everywhere, nor does it quantify HF amount. The correct judgment is to treat HF as a credible hazard and require HF-aware controls/monitoring rather than claiming confirmed or negligible HF generation.
must_include: Surface temperature alone cannot prove internal temperature
HF risk is plausible but not quantified
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming HF definitely was produced in large quantity
Claiming no HF because only surface was measured

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Conservative hazard inference under measurement boundary
analysis.primary_boundary: Surface temperature does not uniquely determine internal decomposition

solver_view.stem: In a sealed test chamber, a lithium-ion cell is externally heated. The cell has a pressure-relief port that opens only when internal pressure exceeds its critical safety pressure. Early in the event, the cell begins venting; later, a much stronger eruption occurs. Select statements that are justified from this information.
options: A Venting implies internal pressure exceeded the port’s critical safety pressure at least momentarily.
B Any venting proves the cell has already entered thermal runaway.
C Describing the event as “initial eruption” followed by “main eruption” is consistent with a two-stage eruption process.
D If venting occurred, the vented material must be only electrolyte vapor.
E Once the port opens, internal pressure cannot rise again.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Gas is forced out when internal pressure exceeds the port’s critical safety pressure.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Venting criterion is pressure threshold; thermal runaway is not established by pressure alone here.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Eruption process is usually divided into initial and main eruption stages.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Only initial vent gases are described as primarily low-boiling electrolyte vapors; venting in general isn’t restricted to that.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Opening condition is stated; no claim that pressure cannot rise again after opening.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Infer what venting can and cannot prove
analysis.primary_boundary: Pressure-triggered venting is not TR proof
disallowed_extrapolations: Equating any venting with thermal runaway
Assuming vent composition fixed for all stages
Assuming pressure behavior after port opening

solver_view.stem: During controlled heating of a lithium-ion cell, gas sampling is taken from the vent line at different times. The cell temperature is estimated from nearby sensors. Select statements that are justified regarding expected dominant gas sources/species at specific temperature ranges.
options: A At 90°C, SEI breakdown can release reducing gases such as H2 and C2H4.
B At 90°C, initial vent gas can primarily be electrolyte vapors such as DMC/DEC/EMC.
C At 150°C, a cathode solid–liquid phase change reaction may have started.
D If H2 is detected at 90°C, it must originate only from SEI breakdown.
E Below 140°C, cathode phase change is impossible because pressure relief cannot occur.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI breaks down at 80–120°C and releases reducing gases including H2 and C2H4.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Initial vent gases primarily consist of low-boiling electrolyte vapors like DMC, DEC, EMC (<140°C).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cathode solid–liquid phase change reaction starts at about 140°C; at 150°C it may have started.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI is one source of H2 here, but exclusivity (only from SEI) is not supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cathode phase change onset and vent opening are distinct; no link that venting cannot occur below 140°C.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Temperature-window reasoning without overclaiming causality
analysis.primary_boundary: Avoid exclusivity and cross-mechanism linkage claims
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming detected species has a single necessary source
Coupling vent-opening physics to cathode phase change onset
Turning 'primarily' into 'only'

solver_view.stem: An engineer proposes a hazard model that treats all venting as a single, uniform phenomenon. In a review, you must decide which statements can be defended about staging and composition using only the following general descriptions: eruptions can occur in an initial and a main stage; initial vent gases are primarily low-boiling electrolyte vapors; SEI breakdown at 80–120°C releases H2 and C2H4. Select defensible critiques.
options: A A two-stage eruption description makes a single-uniform-venting assumption questionable.
B The model must treat initial vent gas as primarily electrolyte vapors rather than cathode-derived products.
C Because SEI releases H2 and C2H4, initial vent gas must be dominated by H2 and C2H4.
D Any appearance of DMC/DEC/EMC proves chamber oxygen is absent.
E The model can ignore pressure-relief port opening because it does not affect whether venting occurs.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Eruption process is usually divided into initial and main eruption, contradicting uniform staging assumptions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Initial vent gases are primarily low-boiling electrolyte vapors such as DMC/DEC/EMC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI can release H2/C2H4, but initial vent is stated primarily as electrolyte vapors, not dominated by those gases.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Presence of electrolyte vapors does not establish chamber oxygen level from the given statements.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Venting through the port occurs when internal pressure exceeds its critical safety pressure; opening affects venting occurrence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Critique modeling assumptions using limited staging/composition facts
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not infer oxygen state or dominance without support
disallowed_extrapolations: Equating one gas source with dominance of vent composition
Inferring chamber oxygen from detected electrolyte vapors
Ignoring pressure-threshold venting trigger

solver_view.prompt: You are writing a simplified, closed-book failure narrative for a heated lithium-ion cell that later erupts. Provide a staged description that is consistent with: (i) a pressure-relief port that vents only after internal pressure exceeds its critical safety pressure, (ii) an initial vs main eruption staging, and (iii) temperature windows for SEI breakdown and cathode phase change. Your answer must state what can be said with confidence and at least one key uncertainty you cannot resolve from these constraints.

ground_truth.gold_answer: A consistent narrative is: as heating progresses, internal pressure can rise until it exceeds the pressure-relief port’s critical safety pressure, forcing gas out through the port. The eruption can be described in an initial stage and a main stage. In the 80–120°C range, SEI breakdown can release reducing gases including H2 and C2H4. Initial vent gas can primarily be low-boiling electrolyte vapors such as DMC/DEC/EMC. Around 140°C and above, a cathode solid–liquid phase change reaction may start. Uncertainty: the exact gas composition at each moment (and dominance of species) cannot be uniquely determined.
must_include: Venting requires exceeding port critical pressure
SEI breakdown 80–120°C releases H2 and C2H4
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming venting proves thermal runaway
Claiming H2 must come only from SEI
Assigning exact compositions to main eruption stage
Inferring oxygen level or combustion state

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Construct defensible narrative while stating unresolved uncertainties
analysis.primary_boundary: Separate what is stated from what is unknowable

solver_view.stem: During four continuous 3C discharge/charge cycles, the highest battery temperature is 43.90 °C and ΔT is 3.46 °C. The design requirement is 20–50 °C and ΔT < 5 °C. Which statements are justified? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The requirement is satisfied for those four continuous 3C cycles.
B The BTMS has enough margin to handle any ambient temperature without exceeding 50 °C.
C The ΔT criterion is satisfied for those four cycles.
D Because ΔT is below 5 °C, the maximum temperature must also be below 50 °C.
E The result proves the coolant flow must have been turbulent in the channels.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: For the stated four 3C cycles, Tmax=43.90 °C lies within 20–50 °C and ΔT<5 °C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Ambient conditions are not specified; margin against arbitrary ambient cannot be concluded.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: ΔT=3.46 °C is explicitly below the 5 °C requirement for those four cycles.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Uniformity (low ΔT) does not logically constrain the absolute temperature; they are separate requirements.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No turbulence claim is given; flow regime needs Reynolds/geometry, not inferred from temperatures alone.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate criteria and avoid causal overreach
analysis.primary_boundary: Cycle-specific observation vs universal operating envelope
disallowed_extrapolations: Any ambient temperature
Turbulence claim without Re-based evidence

solver_view.stem: A coolant loop has maximum flow speed 0.12 m/s, giving a maximum Reynolds number for water of 477.7. Separately, the BTMS keeps Tmax at 43.83 °C at 3C. Which inferences are justified from these statements alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The flow is definitely turbulent because Re is specified.
B The reported Re value is consistent with a laminar-dominated regime in many internal flows.
C The temperature control performance necessarily depends on turbulence to achieve good heat transfer.
D The Re statement alone does not establish channel geometry or hydraulic diameter.
E It is justified to conclude the BTMS will outperform a solid aluminum heat sink at any mass flow.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Re=477.7 is not evidence of turbulence; claiming “definitely turbulent” is unjustified.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A Reynolds number around 478 is generally associated with laminar behavior in many channel/pipe flows.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No linkage is provided between turbulence and achieved Tmax; necessity cannot be concluded.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A Reynolds number value does not uniquely identify geometry; hydraulic diameter/details are not given.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Outperformance vs aluminum heat sink is only stated at the same mass flow and 3C, not all mass flows.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Avoid inferring mechanism from sparse proxies
analysis.primary_boundary: Re and outcome metrics vs design/mechanism certainty
disallowed_extrapolations: Turbulence requirement
Performance dominance at any mass flow

solver_view.prompt: You must decide whether the BTMS, as evidenced by the following results, satisfies a pack-level thermal requirement: 20–50 °C and ΔT < 5 °C. Reported outcomes: (i) at 3C discharge, Tmax = 43.83 °C; (ii) after four continuous 3C discharge/charge cycles, Tmax = 43.90 °C and ΔT = 3.46 °C. State what is justified to claim as “met,” and list two important limitations (what you cannot conclude) without adding new data.

ground_truth.gold_answer: It is justified to claim the maximum-temperature requirement (20–50 °C) is met for the 3C discharge case, and both Tmax and ΔT requirements are met for the four continuous 3C cycles (Tmax 43.90 °C, ΔT 3.46 °C). Limitations: these results do not establish performance at other C-rates or duty cycles, and do not establish compliance under different ambient/pack conditions or any broader safety guarantee.
must_include: Cannot claim ambient-independent or full-safety guarantee
disallowed_extrapolations: Any discharge rate below/above 3C
Vehicle-level safety certification
Ambient-condition independence

analysis.target_cognitive_object: State justified compliance and explicit non-implications
analysis.primary_boundary: Test-point compliance vs operational envelope/safety

solver_view.stem: A 16-cell (18650) module is managed by a BTMS using paraffin PCM with melting range 41–44 °C. Design guidance says keep batteries within 20–50 °C and pack temperature difference within 5 °C. You start at a uniform 40 °C. Which statements are justified from this information alone?
options: A The initial 40 °C condition satisfies the 20–50 °C guidance at t=0.
B A 41–44 °C PCM guarantees all cells stay below 50 °C under any heat load.
C PCM melting range lies inside the stated ideal operating band.
D Meeting the 5 °C temperature-difference rule guarantees no cell exceeds 50 °C.
E If average module temperature is 45 °C, then every cell must be between 42.5–47.5 °C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 40 °C is within the stated 20–50 °C ideal work range.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Melting temperature alone cannot guarantee peak temperatures under arbitrary heat generation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 41–44 °C lies within 20–50 °C, so the phase change range is inside guidance.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A small temperature difference does not bound absolute temperature without a reference level.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 5 °C limit constrains max–min, not deviation from the average.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate absolute limits from uniformity limits
analysis.primary_boundary: PCM melting range is not a safety guarantee
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing max temperature from melting point alone
Inferring all cell temperatures from average

solver_view.stem: A battery module has 16 identical 18650 cells, each with nominal voltage 3.7 V and internal resistance 45 mΩ. No series/parallel wiring is specified. Which conclusions are justified?
options: A Total nominal module energy can be computed exactly from the given data.
B The module must be 16 cells in series, so nominal voltage is 59.2 V.
C If 16 cells were connected in series, total internal resistance would be 0.72 Ω.
D If 16 cells were connected in parallel, equivalent internal resistance would be 2.8125 mΩ.
E The stated internal resistance must be temperature-independent in the 20–50 °C band.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Exact module energy needs the series/parallel configuration and usable voltage window.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Configuration is unspecified; 16 cells could be series, parallel, or mixed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Series resistances add: 16 × 45 mΩ = 720 mΩ = 0.72 Ω.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Parallel resistances divide: 45 mΩ / 16 = 2.8125 mΩ.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No temperature dependence is stated; resistance commonly varies with temperature.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Compute conditional results without assuming topology
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not infer wiring or temperature dependence
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming series string from cell count
Assuming resistance constant across temperatures

solver_view.stem: A BTMS with overall size 97×97×65 mm uses paraffin PCM (41–44 °C melting). An initial condition is t=0, uniform temperature 40 °C, inlet flow velocity 0.01 m/s, and pressure set to 0. Which statements are justified from these specifications alone?
options: A The initial temperature is below the PCM melting range, so initially the PCM is solid.
B Pressure set to 0 implies the system is in vacuum and convection is impossible.
C Flow velocity of 0.01 m/s guarantees temperature differences stay within 5 °C.
D These initial conditions alone determine whether the design will keep 20–50 °C during operation.
E The BTMS geometry being specified is sufficient to compute heat removal without any other properties.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 40 °C is below 41–44 °C, consistent with PCM not yet melted.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A pressure value of 0 can be a gauge reference; vacuum is not established.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Velocity alone cannot ensure temperature uniformity without heat loads and thermal resistances.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Operational temperatures depend on heat generation and boundary conditions not specified.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Geometry alone is insufficient; material properties, flow path, and heat loads are needed.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish initial conditions from performance guarantees
analysis.primary_boundary: Cannot conclude operating limits from initial specs
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring vacuum from pressure=0
Inferring temperature uniformity from velocity only

solver_view.prompt: You must write a conservative requirement statement for a BTMS that uses a paraffin PCM with melting range 41–44 °C, with design guidance to keep batteries within 20–50 °C and temperature difference within 5 °C. The system may start at 40 °C. Write what can be claimed as a design intent, and explicitly state at least two things that cannot be claimed from these specs alone.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Design intent: operate cells within 20–50 °C while maintaining cell-to-cell temperature difference ≤5 °C, using PCM that melts at 41–44 °C; initial uniform condition may be 40 °C. Cannot claim PCM melting guarantees staying <50 °C under all heat loads, nor that initial conditions/geometry/flow speed alone ensure the ≤5 °C uniformity or overall operating compliance.
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing safety/performance under any heat load
Inferring wiring/topology or exact module energy
Inferring vacuum from pressure=0

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Write conservative claims and non-claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Specs define intent, not guaranteed outcomes

solver_view.stem: A vendor proposes adding ~5 wt.% pentadecane to a pouch-cell design as a thermal-runaway retardant (TRR). You are given only these statements: TRR rapidly terminates exothermic reactions; thermal runaway occurs when temperature rises above 90°C; in one impact test on pouch cells, ~5 wt.% pentadecane reduced maximum temperature by ~90%. Which conclusions are justified from this alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The evidence supports that ~5 wt.% pentadecane can reduce peak temperature in at least one pouch-cell impact test.
B It is proven that the modified pouch cell cannot enter thermal runaway under impact because peak temperature drops.
C It is justified to call pentadecane a TRR only if it rapidly terminates exothermic reactions; peak-temperature reduction alone does not prove that mechanism.
D From the impact result, similar percentage peak-temperature reduction should be expected in nail penetration of pouch cells.
E Because thermal runaway occurs above 90°C, a 90% reduction in maximum temperature guarantees the cell stayed below 90°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Direct restatement of the reported impact-test outcome in pouch cells.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Peak-temperature reduction does not prove TR prevention or reaction termination; TR threshold crossing is unknown.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: TRR is defined by rapidly terminating exothermic reactions; temperature reduction alone doesn’t establish that mechanism.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Transfers a pouch impact result to nail penetration in pouch cells without supporting evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Percent reduction doesn’t specify absolute temperatures; maximum could still exceed 90°C.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate observed mitigation from mechanism and safety guarantee
analysis.primary_boundary: Percent peak reduction does not imply staying below threshold
disallowed_extrapolations: generalize impact result to other abuse modes
infer TR elimination from temperature metric alone

solver_view.stem: You must prioritize a single abuse test to quickly screen whether pentadecane shows any thermal mitigation effect, using only the following prior observations: in coin-cell nail penetration, ~4 wt.% pentadecane reduced maximum temperature by ~60%; in pouch-cell impact, ~5 wt.% reduced maximum temperature by ~90%. Which test choice is best supported for screening pouch-cell designs, and why? (Select one.)
options: A Repeat the pouch-cell impact test, because an effect was already demonstrated in that format and abuse type.
B Run only coin-cell nail penetration; results scale directly to pouch cells because wt.% is the same order.
C Run no further tests; a TRR definition alone implies mitigation in any cell once added.
D Run pouch-cell nail penetration; pouch format alone guarantees same mitigation regardless of abuse mode.
E Run coin-cell impact; coin results are more conservative so they must bound pouch behavior.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Only pouch-cell impact mitigation is directly evidenced; it best matches the target design and abuse.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Assumes scaling across formats and abuse modes; coin nail evidence doesn’t directly support pouch screening.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A definition doesn’t establish that pentadecane actually behaves as TRR in cells or reduces temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Format match does not guarantee same response across different failure triggers without evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence that coin-cell impact is more conservative or bounds pouch behavior.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Choose tests consistent with strongest direct evidence
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not assume scaling across format or abuse mode
disallowed_extrapolations: coin-to-pouch transfer without support
nail-to-impact transfer without support

solver_view.stem: A designer argues: “pentadecane diffuses through the electrode stack at ~580 µm/s, so it will rapidly reach internal hot spots and terminate exothermic reactions during an abuse event.” You only know: diffusion rate ~580 µm/s was measured; TRR means rapidly terminates exothermic reactions. Which assessment is most defensible? (Select one.)
options: A The diffusion measurement alone is insufficient to conclude it will terminate exothermic reactions during abuse.
B The diffusion measurement proves pentadecane is a TRR in any lithium-ion cell.
C Because diffusion is fast, impact-test peak-temperature reduction must occur in all cell formats.
D Diffusion rate guarantees thermal runaway will not occur above 90°C.
E Diffusion rate implies pentadecane cannot reduce peak temperature in any abuse test.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Transport speed does not by itself demonstrate reaction termination or effectiveness under abuse conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Conflates diffusion with chemical function; TRR requires terminating exothermic reactions, not just moving quickly.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Generalizes one property to universal temperature mitigation across formats; no evidence supports that transfer.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Doesn’t link diffusion to keeping temperatures below the TR threshold or preventing threshold crossing.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Contradicted by reported temperature reductions in abuse tests using pentadecane.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Avoid inferring functional mitigation from transport property
analysis.primary_boundary: Diffusion rate does not imply reaction-termination efficacy
disallowed_extrapolations: infer TRR behavior from diffusion alone
infer universal mitigation from single metric

solver_view.prompt: You are asked to sign off the statement: “Adding ~5 wt.% pentadecane ensures a pouch cell will not undergo thermal runaway during mechanical abuse.” Using only the information that (i) TRR is defined as rapidly terminating exothermic reactions, (ii) thermal runaway occurs when temperature rises above 90°C, and (iii) abuse tests showed reduced maximum temperature (coin nail: ~60% at ~4 wt.%; pouch impact: ~90% at ~5 wt.%), write a brief technical response: what you can support, and what you cannot conclude.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Supported: pentadecane addition was associated with large reductions in maximum temperature in at least one coin-cell nail penetration test and one pouch-cell impact test. Not supported: that it necessarily prevents thermal runaway, keeps temperature below 90°C, or that it functions as a TRR mechanism (rapid termination of exothermic reactions) from peak-temperature data alone; nor that the same effect transfers to other abuse modes or formats.
must_include: Peak-temperature reduction is evidenced in specified tests
TRR mechanism is not proven by temperature data alone
No guaranteed transfer across abuse modes/formats
disallowed_extrapolations: claiming thermal runaway cannot occur
assuming absolute temperatures from percent reduction
generalizing to all abuse modes or cell types

analysis.target_cognitive_object: State justified claims and explicit non-conclusions
analysis.primary_boundary: Mitigation evidence ≠ safety guarantee or mechanism proof

solver_view.stem: A pack designer proposes: “Use aligned cell arrangement instead of staggered because it has 26.1% higher cooling efficiency and better thermal uniformity.” Which statements are justified from that information alone?
options: A Aligned arrangement can provide higher cooling efficiency and better thermal uniformity than staggered.
B Aligned arrangement will reduce thermal runaway probability in all abuse scenarios.
C Better thermal uniformity implies the maximum cell temperature must always be lower.
D The arrangement advantage could depend on the specific cooling system and boundary conditions.
E The 26.1% improvement guarantees meeting any regulatory temperature limit without redesign.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Direct restatement: aligned showed 26.1% higher cooling efficiency and better thermal uniformity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Overgeneralizes to all abuse scenarios; only cooling/temperature comparison is provided.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Uniformity can improve without reducing absolute peak temperature; peak depends on heat generation and boundary limits.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Engineering judgment: arrangement effects depend on cooling boundary conditions; the claim doesn’t assert universality.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Guaranteeing compliance requires knowing the limit, baseline margin, and operating conditions; not given.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separating comparative thermal metrics from safety guarantees
analysis.primary_boundary: Cooling result doesn’t imply universal TR reduction or compliance
disallowed_extrapolations: TR probability reduction claims
Regulatory compliance guarantees
Universality across cooling designs

solver_view.stem: An engineer states: “Thermal runaway (TR) is a chain exothermic process that can be triggered by mechanical, electrical, or thermal abuse; heat accumulation leads to damage and can trigger combustion or explosion.” Which design statements are supported by this definition-level information?
options: A A design that reduces heat accumulation can be relevant to mitigating TR progression.
B Eliminating overcharge risk alone eliminates TR risk.
C Internal short circuits are one possible electrical trigger for TR.
D TR necessarily ends in explosion in every case.
E Mechanical crushing/collision can be a trigger category for TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: TR is described as involving constant heat accumulation; reducing accumulation is relevant to interrupting progression.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TR has multiple trigger categories; removing one does not eliminate all triggers.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Electrical abuse triggers include internal short circuits.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Definition says combustion or explosion can occur; not that explosion is necessary.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Mechanical abuse triggers include crushing and collision.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Necessary vs possible outcomes; multi-trigger reasoning
analysis.primary_boundary: Definition supports categories, not sufficiency or inevitability
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming one mitigation eliminates TR
Claiming TR always explodes
Adding unlisted trigger categories

solver_view.prompt: You must propose a closed-book, evidence-disciplined argument to a regulator: why a pack-level cooling redesign (e.g., changing cell arrangement) should NOT automatically be claimed to “prevent thermal runaway,” even if it improves cooling efficiency and thermal uniformity. Use only the given statements about TR and the stated cooling comparison. Provide 3–5 sentences.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Thermal runaway is a chain exothermic process that can be triggered by mechanical, electrical (including overcharge and internal short circuits), and thermal abuse; heat accumulation contributes to damage and can lead to combustion or explosion. Improved cooling efficiency and thermal uniformity indicate a thermal-management benefit, but they do not remove non-thermal triggers or guarantee that heat accumulation cannot still occur under other trigger modes. Therefore, the redesign can be claimed to improve temperature control, but not to universally prevent thermal runaway without additional trigger-specific evidence.
must_include: Heat accumulation is part of TR progression
Cooling improvement is not universal TR prevention
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming TR probability reduction is proven
Claiming regulatory compliance is guaranteed
Claiming elimination of mechanical/electrical triggers

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Boundary between thermal metrics and system safety claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Cooling comparison doesn’t prove prevention across triggers

solver_view.stem: A lab shorts a small region inside a Li-ion cell. Instrumentation indicates the local short-site temperature rises to about 110°C, but the cell-level external temperature rise peaks at about 5°C within ~30 s and then plateaus. Which statements are justified from this information alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A Secondary reactions, including electrolyte/electrode decomposition, may be activated at the short site.
B Because the external rise is only ~5°C, no internal decomposition reactions can be occurring.
C The external plateau can be consistent with net heat generation roughly balancing heat transfer at the cell level.
D The cell must inevitably enter thermal runaway once any internal region reaches ~110°C.
E The measurement set is insufficient to conclude whether the cell has a sustained self-heating escalation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: ~110°C at a short site is stated to activate secondary reactions including decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Small external rise does not rule out a localized hot spot reaching ~110°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A temperature plateau is consistent with heat generation and transfer being balanced.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Activation of secondary reactions at ~110°C does not imply inevitable runaway from this information alone.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Given only a brief ~5°C rise and plateau, escalation cannot be concluded either way.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish local activation from system runaway inference
analysis.primary_boundary: Local hotspot data cannot prove global runaway
disallowed_extrapolations: Runaway inevitability from a single temperature threshold

solver_view.stem: An engineer proposes replacing the current collector with a much less conductive, much weaker metal foil to reduce internal short severity. You are told the current collector contributes >90% of electrode electrical conductivity and ~90% of electrode mechanical strength. Which conclusions are justified? (Select all that apply.)
options: A This change will materially reduce electrode electrical conductivity.
B This change will materially reduce electrode mechanical strength.
C The change is guaranteed to improve cell-level safety under all abuse modes.
D The change cannot affect electrical behavior because active material dominates conductivity.
E From these data alone, you cannot conclude the net effect on safety risk is positive or negative.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: >90% conductivity contribution implies degrading collector likely reduces electrode conductivity materially.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: ~90% mechanical strength contribution implies weakening collector likely reduces electrode strength materially.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Component-level conductivity/strength shares do not guarantee a universal safety improvement.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The stem states the collector accounts for >90% of electrode conductivity, contradicting this claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Safety outcome depends on coupled electro-thermal-mechanical effects not specified in the stem.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Avoid system-level safety claims from component shares
analysis.primary_boundary: Safety cannot be inferred from conductivity/strength alone
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteed safety improvement across abuse modes

solver_view.stem: A pouch cell uses a porous polymer separator 10–30 μm thick. During a mechanical intrusion event, the cell shows an abrupt internal short. Which statements are justified from the separator thickness information alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The separator is thin compared with common electrode structural elements, so small through-thickness damage could enable contact.
B You can conclude the intrusion force was low because the separator is only 10–30 μm thick.
C You can conclude the short occurred by separator piercing rather than collector fracture.
D Thickness alone is insufficient to determine the shorting mechanism or the required intrusion energy.
E A 10–30 μm polymer separator guarantees prevention of internal shorts in normal cycling.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A 10–30 μm separator is very thin; limited damage can allow electrode contact.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thickness does not uniquely determine intrusion force; many other mechanics govern required load.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Separator thickness does not identify whether the short came from piercing, tearing, or collector-driven failure.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Only thickness is given; mechanism and energy/load cannot be concluded without more evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports a guarantee against internal shorts during cycling from thickness alone.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate geometric fact from mechanism inference
analysis.primary_boundary: Thickness alone cannot determine cause or force
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring intrusion force or specific short mechanism from thickness

solver_view.prompt: A cell shows a brief external temperature rise that peaks at ~5°C in about 30 s and then plateaus. Separately, diagnostics indicate a local short-site region reached ~110°C. As a safety reviewer in a closed-book setting, state (i) one conclusion you can support about internal reaction activation, and (ii) two important conclusions you cannot support about cell-level outcomes, with a one-sentence rationale for each.

ground_truth.gold_answer: A supported conclusion is that reaching ~110°C at the short site may activate secondary reactions, including electrolyte and electrode decomposition. You cannot conclude that thermal runaway is inevitable, because activation alone does not establish self-heating escalation. You also cannot conclude that no internal decomposition occurred, because a small external rise and plateau can coexist with localized heating and net heat-transfer balance at the cell level.
must_include: Cannot rule out internal reactions from 5°C external rise
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing runaway or guaranteeing safety from the stated temperatures

analysis.target_cognitive_object: State what is supported vs not implied
analysis.primary_boundary: Local temperature vs cell-level outcome inference

solver_view.stem: A pouch Li-ion cell is simulated for nail penetration. Experiments show: some cells enter thermal runaway at a preset load level, while others remain safe even when the load is much higher than expected. Which statements are justified for an engineering modeler interpreting this outcome?
options: A The load level alone is not sufficient to deterministically predict runaway for all cells.
B Thermal runaway must be independent of mechanical loading since some survive high load.
C A model should allow variability/uncertainty beyond a single load threshold to represent observed scatter.
D The nail penetration test proves separator material is isotropic at these conditions.
E The observation implies a monotonic “higher load always safer” trend for this cell design.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Both runaway and non-runaway occur across similar/higher loads, so load alone cannot deterministically predict outcome.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Survival at high load does not prove independence from load; it only shows non-determinism at fixed load.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Observed cell-to-cell scatter requires modeling variability/uncertainty instead of a single critical load.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence links penetration outcomes to isotropy; separator modeling often must consider orthotropy.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The observation contradicts any simple monotonic trend; it shows mixed outcomes, not higher-load safety.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Non-determinism and boundary of threshold inference
analysis.primary_boundary: Cannot infer monotonic or independence from scatter
disallowed_extrapolations: Deriving universal critical load threshold
Concluding load has no effect on TR
Inferring separator isotropy from TR scatter

solver_view.stem: You are choosing the constitutive form for a separator in a coupled electro-thermo-mechanical penetration simulation. Which modeling aspects are explicitly identified as needing consideration when establishing the separator constitutive model?
options: A Orthotropy
B Viscoelasticity
C Temperature dependence
D Thermal shrinkage properties
E Magnetic-field dependence
F Electrolyte ionic conductivity as a constitutive stress term

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Orthotropy is explicitly listed as a required consideration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Viscoelasticity is explicitly listed as a required consideration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Temperature dependence is explicitly listed as a required consideration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Thermal shrinkage properties are explicitly listed as a required consideration.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Magnetic-field dependence is not among the explicitly required separator constitutive aspects given.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The listed constitutive aspects do not include ionic conductivity as a stress constitutive term.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Discriminating stated model requirements vs plausible additions
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not add unstated physics to constitutive scope
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming electromagnetic coupling is required
Treating transport properties as stress terms
Claiming completeness beyond listed aspects

solver_view.stem: You model the separator as an orthotropic solid using Hill’48. For your implementation, which statements are justified about what Hill’48 provides for the separator material description?
options: A It is commonly used to describe orthotropy in a constitutive model.
B It is expressed as a quadratic function of stress components with coefficients (F,G,H,L,M,N) defining anisotropy.
C It guarantees prediction of thermal runaway under nail penetration if calibrated.
D It directly accounts for thermal shrinkage without additional modeling terms.
E It proves the separator is non-porous and blocks electrolyte flow.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Hill’48 is commonly used to describe orthotropy when building constitutive models.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Hill’48 is given as a quadratic stress expression with coefficients F,G,H,L,M,N.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: An orthotropic yield criterion alone cannot guarantee system-level runaway prediction from calibration.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal shrinkage is a separate consideration; Hill’48 orthotropy does not by itself include shrinkage.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Separator is described as porous and supports electrolyte flow; Hill’48 does not imply non-porous behavior.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Model-form capability vs overinterpreting its implications
analysis.primary_boundary: Constitutive orthotropy ≠ thermal/runaway/porosity conclusions
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing TR prediction from yield model
Assuming shrinkage handled without extra terms
Inferring porosity from orthotropy criterion

solver_view.prompt: You must propose a minimal separator material-modeling plan for a penetration simulation. State (i) what the separator is in the cell architecture and (ii) the key constitutive aspects that must be considered. Then state one clear limitation: something you cannot conclude about runaway likelihood using only a single preset load threshold, given that some cells run away at that load while others remain safe at much higher load.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The separator is a porous membrane separating anode and cathode while supporting electrolyte flow. A separator constitutive model must consider orthotropy, viscoelasticity, temperature dependence, and thermal shrinkage. Given mixed outcomes across load levels, a single preset load threshold cannot deterministically predict thermal runaway for all cells.
must_include: Separator is porous; separates anode/cathode; supports electrolyte flow
Cannot claim a universal critical load predicts runaway
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming load has no influence on runaway
Inferring separator isotropy from outcomes
Predicting TR without uncertainty/variability handling

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Translate definitions to modeling plan with boundary limitation
analysis.primary_boundary: Outcome scatter blocks deterministic threshold inference

solver_view.stem: A cell is heated under conditions where its internal temperature can be monitored. The temperature trace shows: (i) a clear onset of detectable self-heating, (ii) later a transition into a self-sustaining uncontrollable temperature rise, and (iii) a final maximum temperature. Select ALL statements that are justified about T1, T2, and T3.
options: A T1 corresponds to onset of detectable self-heating linked to SEI decomposition.
B T2 is the peak temperature and must occur after all gas release ends.
C T2 can be associated with separator failure that leads to an internal short circuit.
D T3 is defined as the highest point on the temperature profile.
E Observing T1 guarantees the cell will later reach T2 and T3.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T1 is defined as detectable self-heating onset due to SEI decomposition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T2 is the trigger temperature, not the peak; gas-ending requirement is unstated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T2 is often associated with separator failure causing an internal short circuit.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: T3 is the peak TR temperature, i.e., highest point on the temperature curve.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T1 indicates self-heating onset but does not guarantee progression to TR trigger and peak.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish definitions vs implied inevitability
analysis.primary_boundary: Onset does not imply eventual runaway
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing TR progression from T1 observation

solver_view.stem: During a safety review, an engineer claims: “Once the battery reaches the thermal runaway trigger temperature T2, the subsequent temperature rise will be self-sustaining and uncontrollable.” Using only the standard meanings of TR and T2/T3, which conclusion is justified?
options: A The claim is justified: T2 equals the definition of thermal runaway behavior.
B The claim is not justified because TR is defined as self-sustaining rise, while T2 is only the trigger temperature for TR.
C The claim is unjustified because separator failure cannot lead to internal short circuit.
D The claim is justified only if T2 is higher than T3.
E No conclusion is possible because T1, T2, and T3 are unrelated to temperature traces.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T2 is where TR is triggered; TR behavior is the runaway process, not merely reaching T2.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: TR is self-sustaining uncontrolled rise; T2 is the trigger temperature, not the definition itself.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states T2 is often associated with separator failure leading to ISC, not the opposite.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T3 is defined as peak temperature; conditioning on T2>T3 is incompatible with that definition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T1–T3 are explicitly defined via the temperature response and profile features.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate trigger points from process definitions
analysis.primary_boundary: Trigger temperature is not sufficient proof of runaway
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating T2 as automatic guarantee of uncontrollable rise

solver_view.stem: A safety engineer writes a pack-level thermal model for a module containing 192 NMC811 18650 cells (total rated energy 2073.6 Wh). The model includes three temperature-triggered exothermic side reactions: SEI decomposition above 363.15 K, negative-electrode/electrolyte above 393.15 K, and positive-electrode/electrolyte above 443.15 K. Which statements are justified from this information alone?
options: A A reaction sequence can begin at 363.15 K due to SEI decomposition being exothermic.
B Thermal runaway is guaranteed once any one of the three thresholds is exceeded.
C The positive-electrode/electrolyte reaction cannot start before 443.15 K in this model.
D The pack will necessarily thermal-runaway because its rated energy exceeds 2 kWh.
E Exceeding 363.15 K proves the temperature rise will be uncontrollable.
F The order of the three initiation thresholds is SEI < negative/electrolyte < positive/electrolyte.
G From these thresholds alone, one can compute the pack peak temperature during runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: SEI decomposition is stated exothermic and starts above 363.15 K, so a sequence can begin there.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal runaway requires an uncontrollable temperature increase; thresholds alone don’t guarantee uncontrollability.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The positive-electrode/electrolyte reaction is initiated only when temperature exceeds 443.15 K.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Rated energy alone does not establish that runaway must occur under any condition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Crossing 363.15 K indicates an exothermic reaction can occur, not that heating becomes uncontrollable.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The three initiation temperatures are explicitly ordered 363.15 K, 393.15 K, then 443.15 K.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Peak temperature needs kinetics, heat release, and heat removal; thresholds alone are insufficient.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Necessary vs sufficient conditions for runaway
analysis.primary_boundary: Thresholds don’t imply uncontrollability or outcomes
disallowed_extrapolations: Predicting runaway occurrence solely from thresholds
Inferring peak temperature without kinetics/heat transfer
Inferring pack safety solely from rated energy

solver_view.stem: You are asked to implement a conservative logic-based detector for potential thermal runaway in a battery management system. Available information: thermal runaway is an uncontrollable temperature increase caused by an exothermic chain reaction; exothermic reactions can initiate above 363.15 K, 393.15 K, and 443.15 K for three mechanisms. Which detector rules are justified without adding unstated assumptions?
options: A Flag “possible onset of exothermic side reactions” if temperature exceeds 363.15 K.
B Flag “thermal runaway confirmed” immediately when temperature exceeds 363.15 K.
C Flag “positive-electrode/electrolyte reaction possible” only if temperature exceeds 443.15 K.
D Do not use any temperature rule because runaway is defined only by temperature rate, not temperature.
E Flag “no risk of runaway” whenever temperature is below 363.15 K.
F Flag “runaway imminent” once temperature exceeds 393.15 K because a chain reaction is guaranteed.
G Flag “pack-level runaway” whenever any single cell exceeds 363.15 K, regardless of pack coupling.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Crossing 363.15 K is explicitly tied to initiating an exothermic SEI decomposition reaction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Runaway requires uncontrollable temperature increase; a threshold crossing alone doesn’t confirm that.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Positive-electrode/electrolyte exothermic reaction is initiated only above 443.15 K.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The provided info includes temperature thresholds for initiating exothermic reactions, so temperature rules can be used for screening.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Below 363.15 K only excludes that specific onset condition; it doesn’t prove no runaway risk from other triggers.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Initiation at 393.15 K doesn’t guarantee an uncontrollable chain reaction or imminence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cell-level onset threshold doesn’t by itself establish pack-level runaway without coupling/propagation conditions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Designing conservative alarms without overclaiming
analysis.primary_boundary: Screening for onset vs confirming runaway
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring pack runaway from a single-cell threshold
Claiming no risk below the lowest threshold
Guaranteeing chain reaction from one onset

solver_view.prompt: A teammate proposes a simplified safety argument: “If our pack never exceeds 363.15 K, thermal runaway cannot occur; if it exceeds 363.15 K, thermal runaway will occur.” Using only the given definition of thermal runaway and the listed initiation thresholds for three exothermic side reactions, write a concise rebuttal that identifies what parts are unsupported and what limited statements are justified.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Thermal runaway is an uncontrollable temperature increase driven by an exothermic chain reaction, not merely exceeding a temperature. Exceeding 363.15 K only supports that SEI decomposition can begin exothermically; it does not prove uncontrollability or inevitability of runaway. Staying below 363.15 K only supports that this SEI-decomposition onset condition is not met; it does not prove runaway is impossible under all circumstances. A justified statement is that additional exothermic mechanisms can initiate only above 393.15 K and 443.15 K, respectively.
must_include: 363.15 K supports onset of SEI decomposition only
393.15 K and 443.15 K are higher onset thresholds
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming inevitability of runaway from threshold exceedance
Claiming absolute safety from staying below 363.15 K
Predicting propagation/pack outcome from onset thresholds alone

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Boundary critique of binary safety arguments
analysis.primary_boundary: Temperature thresholds don’t bracket all runaway conditions

solver_view.stem: A thermal–abuse model for a Panasonic NCR 18650-B (NCA/graphite) defines “ISC triggers” when the jellyroll node temperature reaches 165 °C (separator melting point). In a needle penetration propagation test, one cell in each pack is penetrated by a 2 mm needle at 6 mm/min. Which statements are justified from these facts alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The model uses a temperature threshold at the jellyroll node to trigger ISC.
B In the penetration test, ISC must occur only after the jellyroll reaches 165 °C.
C The penetration protocol specifies needle diameter and loading rate for the penetrated cell.
D The model’s ISC criterion can be directly used as a safety guarantee for the whole pack under penetration.
E If jellyroll temperature stays below 165 °C, penetration cannot create an ISC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: ISC triggering in the model is explicitly defined by jellyroll node reaching 165 °C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Test penetration could create electrical shorts mechanically without requiring the model’s thermal threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Needle diameter and loading rate are explicitly stated for the penetrated cell in propagation tests.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A model trigger condition is not evidence of pack-level safety performance under penetration.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Below-threshold jellyroll temperature does not rule out a mechanically induced short during penetration.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Model criterion vs physical causality under different abuse modes
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not equate model trigger with penetration-induced ISC necessity
disallowed_extrapolations: Pack safety guarantee from ISC trigger definition
Penetration cannot short below 165 °C

solver_view.stem: An engineer wants to reuse an ISC-trigger rule from a thermal model: ISC triggers when jellyroll node temperature reaches 165 °C (separator melting point), calibrated for a Panasonic NCR 18650-B (NCA/graphite, 3350 mAh, 4.2/2.5 V). Which reuse is most defensible without additional validation data?
options: A Apply the same 165 °C ISC trigger to any lithium-ion chemistry because separator melting dominates all ISCs.
B Apply the 165 °C ISC trigger only to the same cell type/model, treating it as a modeling assumption not a universal law.
C Replace 165 °C with 4.2 V because voltage is the direct ISC trigger across all abuse cases.
D Use the 165 °C trigger to infer nail penetration will not cause ISC until separator melting occurs.
E Apply the 165 °C trigger unchanged to any 18650 cell if nominal capacity is within 10% of 3350 mAh.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports universality across chemistries; 165 °C is stated for the chosen battery/model only.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Conservatively confines the criterion to the specific battery/model where it is defined.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cutoff voltages are operating limits, not stated as an ISC triggering condition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Mechanical penetration can create shorts without requiring the model’s thermal threshold condition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Matching capacity does not establish identical separator properties or a valid identical ISC threshold.
