analysis.target_cognitive_object: Safe transfer of a model assumption across designs
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not generalize thresholds beyond specified cell/model
disallowed_extrapolations: Universal 165 °C ISC trigger across chemistries/cells
Voltage cutoff as ISC trigger

solver_view.stem: You must draft a minimal, closed-book test report section describing the cell and one abuse protocol. The only admissible facts are: the cell is Panasonic NCR 18650-B with NCA/graphite; nominal capacity 3350 mAh; cutoff voltages 4.2 V charge and 2.5 V discharge; one cell per pack is penetrated by a 2 mm needle at 6 mm/min. Which statements can be included verbatim without adding assumptions? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The tested cell is an NCA/graphite Panasonic NCR 18650-B.
B The penetration method guarantees repeatable internal short severity across packs.
C The penetrated cell uses a 2 mm diameter needle at 6 mm/min loading rate.
D The cell nominal capacity is 3350 mAh and operates between 4.2 V and 2.5 V cutoffs.
E The pack will undergo thermal runaway propagation once one cell is penetrated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cell identity and electrode chemistry are explicitly specified.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Repeatability of short severity is not stated; protocol details alone do not guarantee it.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Needle diameter and loading rate are explicitly stated for the penetration test.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Nominal capacity and charge/discharge cutoff voltages are explicitly stated.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Propagation outcome is not provided; penetration of one cell does not imply pack propagation.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separating admissible reporting from implied performance claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Protocol description does not imply repeatability or propagation outcomes
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing severity/repeatability from protocol details
Claiming propagation outcome without results

solver_view.prompt: A colleague claims: “Because ISC triggers at 165 °C (separator melting) in our model, preventing the jellyroll from reaching 165 °C ensures a needle penetration at 2 mm and 6 mm/min cannot create an ISC.” Write a brief rebuttal that stays within the stated setup and clearly distinguishes what is defined (model rule) versus what is not established (physical necessity under penetration).

ground_truth.gold_answer: The 165 °C condition is a model-defined ISC trigger based on separator melting at the jellyroll node for this specific cell. A needle penetration protocol (2 mm, 6 mm/min) is a mechanical abuse that can create an internal short by direct damage, which is not shown to require the jellyroll reaching 165 °C. Therefore, the claim that staying below 165 °C prevents penetration-induced ISC is not justified from the stated information.
must_include: 165 °C is a model ISC trigger definition
Penetration is a mechanical abuse protocol
Distinguish model assumption vs physical guarantee
disallowed_extrapolations: Asserting penetration cannot short below 165 °C
Claiming pack-level safety/progression outcomes

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Boundary critique: model trigger vs mechanistic necessity
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not convert a modeling rule into a physical impossibility claim

solver_view.stem: An adiabatic calorimetry test uses these rules: in “Seek” mode, if dT/dt > 0.02 °C/min the system switches to “Exotherm” mode; thermal runaway onset temperature T2 is defined when dT/dt exceeds 100 °C/min. During a run, a cell shows dT/dt = 0.05 °C/min at 70 °C, then 80 °C/min at 240 °C, then 150 °C/min at 253 °C. Select all statements that are justified.
options: A The system should enter “Exotherm” mode by 70 °C.
B T2 occurs at 240 °C because 80 °C/min indicates runaway onset.
C T2 occurs at or above 253 °C in this run.
D Because the end temperature is 300 °C, the peak temperature must be below 300 °C.
E Switching to “Exotherm” mode guarantees T2 will be reached later.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: dT/dt = 0.05 °C/min exceeds 0.02 °C/min, triggering Exotherm mode per rule.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T2 is defined at dT/dt > 100 °C/min; 80 °C/min does not meet the definition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: First time dT/dt exceeds 100 °C/min is at 253 °C, matching T2 definition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A 300 °C end setting does not bound the cell peak; it is a procedural limit, not physics.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Exotherm entry uses 0.02 °C/min; it can occur without ever reaching 100 °C/min.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Threshold logic vs mode changes
analysis.primary_boundary: Mode trigger is not runaway onset
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring peak temperature from procedural end setting

solver_view.stem: A tested cell at 100% SoC has reported values: T1 = 86.8 °C, T2 = 252.7 °C, T3 = 995.8 °C, and maximum temperature rise rate dT/dt_max = 8640 °C/min. T2 is defined as the temperature where dT/dt exceeds 100 °C/min. Select all statements that are consistent with these definitions and values.
options: A At T2, the temperature rise rate must be greater than 100 °C/min.
B Because dT/dt_max is 8640 °C/min, the cell must have reached Exotherm mode before T2.
C The value T3 = 995.8 °C is compatible with an end temperature setting of 300 °C only if the cell can self-heat above the setpoint.
D T1 must be defined by the same 0.02 °C/min criterion used to enter Exotherm mode.
E From these values alone, one can conclude the cell is safer at 50% SoC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly follows from the stated definition of T2 at dT/dt exceeding 100 °C/min.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: dT/dt_max alone does not prove when 0.02 °C/min was first exceeded relative to T2.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Given a 300 °C procedural end, observing 995.8 °C implies self-heating beyond that setting.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No definition for T1 is provided; assigning the 0.02 °C/min threshold to T1 is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Only 100% SoC data are given; concluding 50% SoC safety is an extrapolation beyond evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: What can and cannot be concluded
analysis.primary_boundary: Single-condition data cannot rank other SoC
disallowed_extrapolations: SoC safety ranking from 100% SoC only

solver_view.stem: An adiabatic test procedure is configured with: initial target temperature 40 °C, end temperature 300 °C, temperature step 5 °C, waiting 30 min, searching 20 min. The system enters “Exotherm” when dT/dt in “Seek” exceeds 0.02 °C/min. Which statement is the most defensible engineering interpretation of what this configuration controls?
options: A It constrains the chamber temperature program and detection cadence, not the cell’s ultimate peak temperature.
B It guarantees thermal runaway will be detected before any venting occurs.
C It fixes T2 at a value below 300 °C for any cell chemistry.
D It ensures the cell is always in thermal equilibrium at each 5 °C step.
E It makes the onset criterion (100 °C/min) unnecessary because Exotherm mode is equivalent to runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The listed settings are procedural targets/timings; they do not physically cap the cell’s self-heated peak.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence links this configuration to venting timing or guarantees detection before venting.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: T2 is defined by dT/dt > 100 °C/min, not by the programmed end temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Waiting/search times do not prove full equilibrium; equilibrium depends on thermal time constants not provided.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Exotherm trigger uses 0.02 °C/min, while runaway onset uses 100 °C/min; they are not equivalent.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Procedural settings vs physical outcomes
analysis.primary_boundary: Test program does not guarantee phenomena timing
disallowed_extrapolations: Equating procedural end temperature with cell peak

solver_view.prompt: You receive a temperature-time trace from an adiabatic run but no plots of mode states. The only rules you are allowed to use are: (i) the system enters “Exotherm” when dT/dt in “Seek” exceeds 0.02 °C/min, and (ii) thermal runaway onset temperature T2 is defined when dT/dt exceeds 100 °C/min. Explain how you would determine (a) the earliest temperature at which Exotherm mode should have been triggered and (b) T2, and state one conclusion you should NOT draw from knowing the procedure’s end temperature is 300 °C.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Identify the first temperature where the measured dT/dt exceeds 0.02 °C/min (in Seek) as the point Exotherm mode should trigger. Identify T2 as the first temperature where dT/dt exceeds 100 °C/min. Do not conclude the cell’s peak temperature must be below 300 °C; the 300 °C value is a procedural end setting, not a physical cap.
must_include: Do not cap peak temperature at 300 °C
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring safety ranking across SoC
Assuming Exotherm implies runaway onset

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Event identification from rate thresholds
analysis.primary_boundary: Procedure settings are not physical bounds

solver_view.stem: A coupled simulation of a commercial LCO/graphite pouch cell uses a separator failure criterion: an electrical short circuit is activated when thickness-direction separator strain exceeds a failure strain (εz > εf). Which statements are justified from this setup alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The short-circuit trigger is explicitly tied to exceeding εf in separator thickness strain.
B Any event with εz > εf will necessarily lead to thermal runaway in the model.
C The setup implies a mechanical module must supply εz to decide if a short activates.
D The criterion guarantees the first short location is where separator porosity is minimum.
E The criterion proves the model can predict nail penetration outcomes without recalibration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The short is activated when εz exceeds εf as stated.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Short activation does not, by itself, imply runaway inevitability.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A mechanical model is part of the coupled model, enabling strain evaluation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No rule is stated linking initiation location to minimum porosity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports generalizing to nail penetration or calibration needs.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Trigger criterion vs outcome inevitability
analysis.primary_boundary: Activation rule does not imply runaway
disallowed_extrapolations: TR inevitability from short activation
Predicting other abuse modes without evidence
Inferring hotspot location from porosity fit alone

solver_view.stem: In a model of an LCO/graphite pouch cell, separator porosity under compression is fitted as p* = p0 + 0.05118εz − 0.00858εz^2. Which conclusions are justified without assuming any numeric value of p0 or εz range? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The fit is non-linear in εz, so porosity change with strain need not be monotonic.
B For sufficiently large εz, the fitted p* will decrease with further εz.
C The fit guarantees porosity stays between 0 and 1 for all εz.
D The porosity is maximized at εz = 0 because p* equals p0 there.
E The fit alone proves compression always reduces ionic resistance.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Quadratic dependence allows non-monotonic behavior in principle.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Negative quadratic term implies eventual negative slope for large εz.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No constraint is provided enforcing physical bounds on p*.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Maximum location depends on coefficients; εz=0 need not be maximal.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Porosity-to-resistance direction is not stated; could increase resistance.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Nonlinearity vs physical guarantees
analysis.primary_boundary: No bounds/transport link given
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming p* remains physical for all strains
Inferring resistance trends from porosity fit
Assuming strain range or sign

solver_view.stem: A multiphysics model for a commercial LCO/graphite pouch cell is described as coupled mechanical, electrochemical, and thermal modules. Which statements are justified purely from that description? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The model architecture can represent two-way coupling among mechanics, electrochemistry, and heat.
B The model must include chemical side-reaction kinetics for thermal runaway prediction.
C The model could, in principle, pass mechanical outputs (e.g., strain) to affect electrical behavior.
D The mesh size alone guarantees accurate prediction of short-circuit onset.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Side-reaction kinetics are not stated as required components.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Coupled modules allow mechanical quantities to influence electrochemical behavior.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Element count does not, by itself, guarantee accuracy for onset prediction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Chemistry-specific results cannot be generalized to other cathodes from this alone.

disallowed_extrapolations: Accuracy guarantees from mesh count
Generalizing across chemistries
Assuming side-reaction kinetics exist

solver_view.prompt: You are auditing a coupled mechanical–electrochemical–thermal model of an LCO/graphite pouch cell where a short circuit is activated when separator thickness strain exceeds εf, and separator porosity is fitted as p* = p0 + 0.05118εz − 0.00858εz^2. State two conclusions you can defend and two conclusions you cannot defend from this information alone, focusing on boundary/overreach.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Defensible: (1) the short-circuit trigger is explicitly εz > εf; (2) porosity is modeled as a nonlinear (quadratic) function of εz within a coupled multiphysics framework. Not defensible: (1) that short activation necessarily causes thermal runaway; (2) that porosity remains physically bounded or that compression necessarily reduces ionic resistance / guarantees onset prediction accuracy or transferability to other chemistries.

At least one “cannot conclude physical bounds/transport effect”

solver_view.stem: A 2000 mAh pouch Li-ion cell is heated while held in a constant-force squeezing fixture. option_judgments.label: Incorrect

solver_view.prompt: You must design an instrumentation-only criterion to flag imminent failure during a heated squeezing test on 2000 mAh pouch Li-ion cells. The only qualitative behavior you are allowed to use is: thickness decreases slowly with increasing temperature and drops abruptly at TR onset. ground_truth.gold_answer: Alert criterion: monitor thickness versus time/temperature and trigger an alarm/shutdown when a rapid, abrupt thickness drop occurs, since this coincides with TR onset. Limitation: thickness change alone cannot identify the initiating mechanism or guarantee TR prevention.
must_include: Use abrupt thickness drop as trigger

For cases (i) and (iii), internal peak temperature and the maximum internal–surface temperature difference were recorded. Which statements are justified by these observations alone? (Select all that apply.)

B Surface peak temperature must be higher at 100%SOC than at 50%SOC.

option_judgments.justification: Max internal–surface differences are stated: 256.3°C (100%SOC) vs 92.9°C (50%SOC).

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Data include only specific cases; cannot generalize to any nail depth or probability of TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Maximum internal–surface difference reaches 256.3°C in full depth@100%SOC, exceeding 200°C.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate what is measured vs inferred
analysis.primary_boundary: Surface behavior and general TR risk not given
disallowed_extrapolations: Any-depth SOC effect on TR likelihood
Surface peak temperature ordering
Mechanistic causes of temperature gap

solver_view.stem: In nail-penetration tests on an NCM811 cell, two cases are compared: full depth@100%SOC and full depth@50%SOC. Observed jet metrics: maximum jet angle 72.0° vs 47.9°, and jet area duty ratio 41.2% vs 17.8% (100%SOC first). Which conclusions are justified from these numbers alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A At full depth, 100%SOC produced a larger maximum jet angle than 50%SOC.
B At full depth, 100%SOC produced a higher jet area duty ratio than 50%SOC.
C The jet is necessarily more hazardous at 100%SOC because it lasts longer in time.
D The larger maximum jet angle implies higher internal peak temperature in that case.
E These jet metrics alone prove the half-depth@100%SOC case is safer than full-depth@50%SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Maximum jet angle is stated: 72.0° (100%SOC) vs 47.9° (50%SOC).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Jet area duty ratio is stated: 41.2% (100%SOC) vs 17.8% (50%SOC).

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A duty ratio is given, but no absolute duration or hazard criterion is provided.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Jet angle does not logically determine internal peak temperature without additional coupling evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No jet/temperature metrics are provided for half depth@100%SOC, so cross-case safety ranking is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Avoid turning metrics into safety proof
analysis.primary_boundary: Hazard and duration not defined; half-depth lacks outputs
disallowed_extrapolations: Safety ranking across unmeasured case
Inferring duration from duty ratio alone
Inferring temperature from jet angle

solver_view.stem: For nail-triggered thermal runaway on an NCM811 cell, internal peak temperatures were 887.3°C (full depth@100%SOC) and 483.2°C (full depth@50%SOC). Maximum internal–surface temperature differences were 256.3°C and 92.9°C, respectively. Which statements are defensible? (Select all that apply.)
options: A Surface temperature can substantially under-report internal severity during runaway.
B In both cases, the surface temperature exceeded the internal temperature at some point.
C The surface temperature at full depth@100%SOC could be at least 256.3°C lower than the internal peak at some moment.
D Because internal peaks differ by over 400°C, the released chemical energy must be more than doubled at 100%SOC.
E The larger temperature difference at 100%SOC could reflect stronger internal localization of heat than at 50%SOC, without contradicting the data.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Reported maximum internal–surface gaps (256.3°C, 92.9°C) show surface can be far below internal.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Data only report maximum internal-minus-surface difference, not any time when surface exceeded internal.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A maximum internal–surface difference of 256.3°C implies a moment with internal at least that much above surface.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Temperature peak differences do not uniquely determine released chemical energy without mass, heat losses, and kinetics.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Greater internal–surface gap at 100%SOC is consistent with more localized internal heating; mechanism not asserted as proven.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interpret temperature gaps without overclaiming mechanisms
analysis.primary_boundary: Energy release and time histories not provided
disallowed_extrapolations: Quantifying energy release from peak temperatures
Claiming surface>internal occurred
Generalizing to other geometries or cells

solver_view.prompt: You must design a closed-loop safety trigger during nail penetration using only a surface temperature sensor. In one tested case, the maximum internal–surface temperature difference reached 256.3°C, with an internal peak of 887.3°C (full depth@100%SOC). In another, the maximum difference was 92.9°C with an internal peak of 483.2°C (full depth@50%SOC). State (i) one conservative design implication for surface-temperature-only triggers, and (ii) one specific conclusion you are NOT justified to make about surface peak temperatures or overall safety across nail depths.

ground_truth.gold_answer: (i) Surface readings can lag far below internal conditions; a trigger based only on surface temperature must assume potentially large underestimation (up to the observed 256.3°C gap in these tests). (ii) You cannot conclude the surface peak temperature ordering between 100% and 50% SOC, nor rank safety across different nail depths (e.g., half depth vs full depth) from the given data.
must_include: Cannot infer surface peak temperature ordering
Cannot rank safety across nail depths
disallowed_extrapolations: Any-depth SOC effect on TR probability
Deriving surface peaks numerically
Inferring hazard solely from jet metrics

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Design under sensor boundary and inference limits
analysis.primary_boundary: Surface sensor lacks internal state; missing half-depth outcomes

solver_view.stem: A 1000 mAh pouch cell at 298.15 K is subjected to nail penetration. Observations are summarized as four processes: nail penetration, internal short circuit, chemical reaction, and thermal runaway. A separate criterion used in the lab is: if cell temperature exceeds 506.15 K, thermal runaway occurs for all cells. Which statements are justified?
options: A Thermal runaway may occur after nail penetration and internal short circuit, following chemical reaction as a process stage.
B Thermal runaway cannot occur unless the nail remains inserted until the end of the test.
C If any point in the cell exceeds 506.15 K, thermal runaway is expected for all tested cells.
D Because tests start at 298.15 K, nail penetration alone cannot initiate thermal runaway.
E The four-process description proves the internal short circuit is the dominant heat source during thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Restates the observed sequence of processes during nail penetration without adding mechanism or timing detail.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Nail remaining inserted is an unstated condition; the process list does not require it.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly matches the stated threshold rule: exceeding 506.15 K leads to thermal runaway for all cells.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Starting at room temperature does not rule out rapid heating to the threshold during abuse.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A stage list does not identify dominant heat source; dominance is not provided.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate observed stages from implied causality
analysis.primary_boundary: Stage order does not prove dominance or necessity
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring dominant heat source from stage listing
Adding constraints about nail dwell time
Concluding initiation impossibility from ambient start

solver_view.stem: A pouch cell has a cathode composed of 70% lithium cobalt oxide and 30% ternary material, nominal capacity 1000 mAh. Nail penetration tests are conducted at 298.15 K. Select the statements that are supported without additional assumptions.
options: A The cathode contains both lithium cobalt oxide and a ternary material fraction.
B The cathode composition alone determines whether nail penetration leads to thermal runaway.
C The nominal capacity fixes the total heat release during thermal runaway.
D The experiments were performed at 298.15 K initial ambient/room temperature.
E The 70/30 cathode blend guarantees higher abuse tolerance than a single-material cathode.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Direct restatement of the given cathode composition fractions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Determinism is not supported; other factors and processes are described without isolating composition as sole driver.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Capacity does not uniquely determine heat release; chemistry and reaction extent are not provided.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly matches the stated experimental temperature condition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No comparative abuse-tolerance data versus other cathodes is provided; guarantee is unjustified.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identify what parameters do and do not imply
analysis.primary_boundary: No performance or causality from composition/capacity alone
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring TR determinism from cathode blend
Inferring heat release from capacity alone
Claiming guaranteed tolerance improvements

solver_view.prompt: You are asked to write a conservative safety statement for nail-penetration testing of this 1000 mAh pouch cell at 298.15 K. Use only the given facts: (i) nail penetration involves four processes (nail penetration, internal short circuit, chemical reaction, thermal runaway), and (ii) if cell temperature exceeds 506.15 K, thermal runaway occurs for all cells. Provide a short statement of what can be claimed and one explicit limitation (what cannot be concluded).

ground_truth.gold_answer: Claim: During nail penetration the cell can progress through nail penetration, internal short circuit, chemical reaction, and thermal runaway; and if the cell temperature exceeds 506.15 K, thermal runaway occurs for all cells tested. Limitation: these facts do not identify the dominant heat source or guarantee prevention by any single design/condition below the threshold.
must_include: 506.15 K exceedance implies TR for all cells
disallowed_extrapolations: Stating nail must remain inserted
Claiming composition/capacity ensures safety
Asserting dominant heat source or prevention method

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Formulate bounded safety claim plus limitation
analysis.primary_boundary: Threshold and stage list do not imply mechanism dominance

solver_view.stem: An 18650 cell is monitored during a fast mechanical abuse event. Surface thermography is recorded at 30 Hz. A single type-K Inconel-sheathed thermocouple is also used, logged at 1 Hz. You want to identify brief thermal spikes (duration ~0.05 s) that may occur on the cell surface. Which statements are justified?
options: A The 30 Hz thermography is capable of time-resolving ~0.05 s spikes better than the 1 Hz thermocouple log.
B The thermocouple measurement necessarily has higher temperature accuracy than the thermal camera because it is a contact sensor.
C With only 1 Hz logging, the thermocouple record can miss a 0.05 s spike even if the thermocouple junction physically heats up.
D The ±0.001 °C resolution guarantees the thermocouple will detect any spike regardless of its duration.
E If both sensors show no spike, it proves no spike occurred anywhere on the cell.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 30 Hz sampling provides finer time discretization than 1 Hz for sub-second spikes.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Contact vs non-contact does not, by itself, establish higher accuracy; accuracy is not specified.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 1 Hz, events between samples can be missed despite physical heating.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: High resolution does not overcome undersampling; duration can still be missed at 1 Hz.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No-spike readings do not prove absence everywhere; single TC and surface imaging have coverage limits.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Sampling limits vs sensor resolution/coverage
analysis.primary_boundary: Inferring absence from finite-rate measurements
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming internal absence from surface/one-point data
Claiming accuracy hierarchy not stated

solver_view.stem: Two labs run destructive impact tests on the same 18650 cell type. Both use the same type-K Inconel-sheathed thermocouples. Lab A logs temperature at 1 Hz; Lab B at 10 Hz. Which conclusions are justified purely from this information?
options: A Lab B can capture faster temperature transients in its recorded data than Lab A.
B Lab A and Lab B will necessarily report the same peak temperature because they use the same thermocouple type.
C Lab B will necessarily measure higher peak temperatures because higher sampling increases real heating.
D Lab A's ±0.001 °C resolution guarantees it will be more reliable for peak detection than Lab B.
E From sampling rate alone, you can conclude Lab B's thermal runaway risk assessment is more accurate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Higher sampling rate reduces the chance of missing short-lived peaks in the recorded signal.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Same sensor type does not ensure identical recorded peaks; sampling and placement can differ.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Sampling rate affects observation, not the physical heating of the cell.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Resolution is not sufficient for peak detection if peaks occur between 1 Hz samples.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Risk assessment accuracy depends on more than sampling rate; no such linkage is given.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate measurement fidelity from physical outcome
analysis.primary_boundary: Sampling rate ≠ safety/risk validity
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding TR risk accuracy from sampling alone
Assuming identical peaks from same sensor type

solver_view.stem: A cylindrical cell used in a mechanical abuse test has diameter 18 mm and height 65 mm. Which statements are justified without any additional information?
options: A The cell is consistent with the standard 18650 cylindrical form factor.
B Its rated capacity is necessarily 2.5 Ah because it is 18650.
C If the nominal voltage is 3.7 V, the chemistry must be NMC.
D The cell's maximum voltage must be 4.2 V for safe operation.
E From geometry alone, you can conclude the cell has lower thermal runaway risk than a pouch cell.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 18 mm diameter and 65 mm height matches the 18650 geometric convention stated.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Capacity is not implied by form factor; many 18650 capacities exist.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A 3.7 V nominal voltage does not uniquely identify chemistry.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Maximum voltage is a specification, not guaranteed by geometry; it must be provided explicitly.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal runaway risk cannot be concluded from geometry alone; many design and test factors matter.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Non-implicability from form factor
analysis.primary_boundary: Geometry does not determine specs or safety
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring capacity/chemistry/limits from size
Inferring TR risk from form factor

solver_view.prompt: You are asked to use two instruments during a destructive mechanical abuse test of an 18650 cell: (i) surface thermography recorded at 30 Hz, and (ii) a single type-K Inconel-sheathed thermocouple logged at 1 Hz with ±0.001 °C resolution. In 4–6 sentences, state what you can and cannot legitimately conclude about brief, localized temperature spikes, and why. Do not assume any additional sensor placement details.

ground_truth.gold_answer: You can conclude the thermography has finer time sampling (30 Hz) than the 1 Hz thermocouple log, so short spikes are less likely to be missed in the recorded thermography. You cannot conclude that either instrument is intrinsically more accurate in absolute temperature without calibration/uncertainty information. A 1 Hz log can miss brief spikes even if the thermocouple junction physically heats. If neither sensor shows a spike, you still cannot prove no localized spike occurred, because coverage is limited (single point thermocouple and surface-only imaging).
must_include: 30 Hz vs 1 Hz affects transient capture
Accuracy not implied by sensor type
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming internal temperatures are known from these sensors
Claiming absolute accuracy hierarchy without stated uncertainty
Claiming safety/risk conclusions from sampling rates alone

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Measurement limits: sampling, coverage, and inference
analysis.primary_boundary: Observed signal absence ≠ physical absence

solver_view.stem: A lab measures flammability limits (LFL/UFL) of a battery vent-gas mixture in a 5 L spherical explosion vessel at 300 K and 100 kPa. Flammability is defined as the critical fuel concentration for flame propagation. The lab declares “ignition” when vessel pressure rises by 7%. Which conclusions are justified from this information?
options: A The reported LFL and UFL are conditional on the stated vessel, pressure-rise ignition criterion, and initial conditions.
B The results can be directly used to set safe limits for any room-sized enclosure without additional validation.
C A 7% pressure rise is an operational ignition criterion; it is not inherently the universal physical definition of ignition.
D Because the vessel is spherical, the measured limits are geometry-independent and transferable to all chamber shapes.
E The measured limits necessarily imply the mixture will always detonate if within LFL–UFL.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Limits were measured in a specified 5 L vessel at 300 K, 100 kPa using a 7% pressure-rise ignition rule.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Scale-up to room enclosures is not established by vessel FL measurements or definitions given.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Ignition here is explicitly defined by a 7% pressure-rise criterion, indicating a test-specific operational definition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Using a spherical vessel does not prove geometry-independence for other chambers; no such transfer claim is supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Flammability limits refer to flame propagation, not an inevitability of detonation within the range.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Test-definition vs real-world hazard inference
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not scale vessel FLs to enclosures
disallowed_extrapolations: Room safety limits from 5 L vessel
Detonation inevitability from flammability

solver_view.stem: At 300 K and 100 kPa, a vent-gas mixture shows the following trends: when H2 volume concentration increases from 10% to 30%, LFL decreases from 15.65% to 12.3% and UFL increases from 50.6% to 56.92%. When CO increases from 5% to 50%, LFL decreases from 14.8% to 12.71% and UFL increases from 44.85% to 54.6%. Which statements are supported?
options: A Increasing either H2 or CO (over the stated ranges) widens the flammable concentration window.
B Increasing either H2 or CO (over the stated ranges) raises the LFL and lowers the UFL.
C With H2 increased from 10% to 30%, the LFL decreases while UFL increases under the stated conditions.
D With CO increased from 5% to 50%, the LFL decreases while UFL increases under the stated conditions.
E These trends guarantee the same LFL/UFL shifts at any initial temperature and pressure.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Both cases show LFL decreases and UFL increases, which enlarges the interval between them for stated conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The reported direction is opposite: LFL decreases and UFL increases for both H2 and CO changes.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The H2 variation explicitly shows LFL 15.65%→12.3% and UFL 50.6%→56.92% at 300 K, 100 kPa.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The CO variation explicitly shows LFL 14.8%→12.71% and UFL 44.85%→54.6% at 300 K, 100 kPa.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The trends are only stated for 300 K and 100 kPa; extending to other conditions is not supported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Nonlinear window-widening trend recognition
analysis.primary_boundary: No extrapolation beyond 300 K, 100 kPa
disallowed_extrapolations: Any-temperature/pressure generality

solver_view.stem: A team uses a 5 L explosion vessel to determine whether a gas mixture is “ignited.” They mark ignition when internal vessel pressure rises by 7%. Flammability limit is defined as the critical fuel concentration for flame propagation (LFL/UFL). Which statement is the most defensible engineering interpretation of what the 7% criterion controls?
options: A It defines the operational detection threshold for ignition in that apparatus, not necessarily the minimum energy needed physically.
B It proves the mixture releases exactly 7% more chemical energy than baseline at ignition.
C It guarantees the flame has propagated through the entire vessel volume whenever 7% is exceeded.
D It is equivalent to the universal lower flammability limit definition.
E It implies the measured LFL/UFL are independent of sensor response time.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The 7% pressure rise is explicitly a criterion used to consider the mixed gas ignited in the vessel.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A pressure-rise threshold does not specify a precise energy increase; no energy quantification is given.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The criterion indicates ignition detection, not complete flame traversal; flame propagation extent is not stated.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LFL/UFL are defined via critical concentration for flame propagation, not by a pressure-rise percentage.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No information is provided about sensor dynamics; independence from response time cannot be concluded.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Proxy metric vs physical state interpretation
analysis.primary_boundary: Pressure-rise threshold is apparatus-specific
disallowed_extrapolations: Energy release magnitude from 7% pressure
Flame completeness from threshold exceedance

solver_view.prompt: You are asked to use measured LFL/UFL values of battery vent gas obtained in a 5 L spherical explosion vessel at 300 K and 100 kPa. Ignition in that setup is declared when pressure rises by 7%. Write a brief expert note stating (i) what these results do support operationally, and (ii) two specific claims that would be unjustified if someone tried to apply them directly to real enclosures or other conditions.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The results support an apparatus- and condition-specific determination of flammability limits (LFL/UFL) as critical fuel concentrations for flame propagation, using a 7% vessel pressure-rise as the ignition criterion at 300 K and 100 kPa in a 5 L sphere. Unjustified claims include: that the same limits apply unchanged in room-scale or differently shaped enclosures, and that the same trends/limits hold at other initial temperatures or pressures (or imply detonation inevitability).
must_include: FLs are critical concentrations for flame propagation
Reject transfer to other scales or conditions
disallowed_extrapolations: Room-enclosure safety limits from 5 L vessel results
All-temperature/all-pressure generalization
Detonation inevitability within LFL–UFL

analysis.target_cognitive_object: State what is supported vs not implied
analysis.primary_boundary: No scale-up or regime generalization

solver_view.stem: A materials team decorates an InSe monolayer with CuO. They report the band gap changes from 1.938 eV (pristine InSe) to 0.831 eV (CuO–InSe). They also compute a binding energy Eb = −2.133 eV and use the rule: negative Eb implies thermodynamic/structural stability, and more negative means higher stability. Which conclusions are justified from only these statements?
options: A. CuO decoration reduces the band gap relative to pristine InSe.
B. CuO–InSe will be metallic because the band gap decreased strongly.
C. The computed decoration is thermodynamically and structurally stable by the given stability rule.
D. CuO–InSe is more stable than any other possible decoration on InSe.
E. At 400 K, the decoration must remain stable because Eb is negative at 0 K.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly restates the reported band-gap decrease after CuO decoration.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A reduced band gap does not imply zero gap; metallicity is not established.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Given rule states negative Eb indicates thermodynamic/structural stability; Eb is negative.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No comparison to other decorations is provided; cannot claim global maximum stability.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Temperature-dependent stability at 400 K cannot be concluded from Eb alone.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish supported conclusions from tempting stability/extrapolation claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Eb and band gap do not imply finite-temperature behavior
disallowed_extrapolations: Any claim of 400 K stability solely from Eb
Any claim of metallic state without zero band gap evidence
Any claim of best-in-class stability without comparisons

solver_view.stem: A report defines band gap for a semiconductor as the energy difference between the conduction band minimum (CBM: lowest energy above the Fermi level) and valence band maximum (VBM: highest energy below the Fermi level). Using only this definition, which statements are necessarily consistent with calling a material “semiconducting” in that report’s sense?
options: A. The CBM lies at a higher energy than the VBM.
B. The CBM must occur at the same k-point as the VBM.
C. The band gap equals (energy of CBM) minus (energy of VBM).
D. The Fermi level must lie exactly midway between CBM and VBM.
E. A smaller band gap guarantees higher thermodynamic stability.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CBM is defined as above the Fermi level and VBM below; CBM energy exceeds VBM energy.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Definition does not require direct or indirect gap; k-point relation is unstated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Restates the provided band-gap definition as CBM–VBM energy difference.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Mid-gap Fermi level is not implied by the band-gap definition alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Stability is linked to binding energy rule, not band-gap size; no causal link given.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Apply definitions without importing extra semiconductor assumptions
analysis.primary_boundary: Definition alone does not fix k-space or Fermi-level position
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring direct vs indirect gap
Inferring Fermi level location beyond 'between bands'
Inferring stability from band gap magnitude

solver_view.stem: AIMD simulations were run for pristine InSe and CuO–InSe at 300 K and 400 K. Separately, a binding energy Eb = −2.133 eV is reported with the rule: negative Eb indicates thermodynamic/structural stability (more negative = more stable). Which claims are justified from only these statements?
options: A. AIMD was used to probe behavior at both 300 K and 400 K for both materials.
B. AIMD at 400 K proves the decoration is stable for all higher temperatures.
C. The negative Eb supports that the decoration is stable under the stated stability rule.
D. AIMD results are unnecessary because Eb alone fully determines finite-temperature stability.
E. The decoration must increase the band gap at 400 K compared to 300 K.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly restates that AIMD was performed on both systems at 300 K and 400 K.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Two temperatures cannot justify stability at all higher temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Given rule links negative Eb to stability, and Eb is negative.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Statement overclaims; Eb rule does not establish finite-temperature stability completeness.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No temperature trend of band gap is provided; cannot infer direction at 400 K.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate what simulations imply from what they do not
analysis.primary_boundary: Limited temperatures do not imply universal stability or properties
disallowed_extrapolations: Stability beyond 300–400 K based on AIMD existence
Finite-temperature stability fully determined by Eb
Band-gap temperature dependence without stated data

solver_view.prompt: You must brief a safety-critical design review using only these facts: CuO decoration changes InSe band gap from 1.938 eV to 0.831 eV; Eb for CuO–InSe is −2.133 eV; negative Eb indicates thermodynamic/structural stability (more negative = more stable); AIMD was performed at 300 K and 400 K for pristine InSe and CuO–InSe. Write the most defensible conclusion you can, and list two specific conclusions you must NOT claim.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Defensible: CuO decoration is reported to reduce the InSe band gap (1.938→0.831 eV) and has a negative binding energy (−2.133 eV), which by the stated rule indicates a thermodynamically/structurally stable decoration; AIMD was conducted at 300 K and 400 K for both systems. Must NOT claim: that the system is metallic or that stability is guaranteed outside the stated conditions (e.g., all higher temperatures) or is best among all decorations.
must_include: Band gap decreases with CuO decoration
Eb is negative and implies stability per rule
AIMD performed at 300 K and 400 K
Two disallowed claims (metallic / universal stability / best stability)
disallowed_extrapolations: Metallicity conclusion from band-gap decrease
Stability at all temperatures from Eb or AIMD
Comparative “most stable” claim vs other decorations

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Compose conservative conclusions and explicit non-claims
