solver_view.stem: A sealed chamber test heats the same NCM811 cylindrical cell using either a rod, coil, or plate heater. All tests use constant heater power 265 W and stop heating when thermal runaway (TR) begins. Observations: with the coil, TR begins after 225 s and the temperature at TR onset is 140.07 °C, which is higher than for rod and plate tests. Which statements are justified?
options: A TR onset temperature depends on heater geometry, not only heater power.
B The coil is safer because it delays TR and raises TR onset temperature.
C Under identical power control, different heaters can change time-to-TR.
D The coil deposits more total energy before TR than rod and plate.
E The rod must have triggered TR at 91.13 °C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Different heater types at same power led to different TR onset temperatures; geometry/technique matters beyond power alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: “Safer” is a system-level claim not supported by only onset time/temperature under one chamber protocol.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Coil TR at 225 s; other heaters differ in onset temperature, implying heater choice can shift onset timing.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Energy-before-TR depends on each heater’s time-to-TR, which is not given for rod/plate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Rod onset temperature is not stated; computing 140.07−48.94 assumes the temperature difference refers to rod specifically without ambiguity.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate supported comparisons from safety conclusions
analysis.primary_boundary: Do not infer safety/energy without missing times
disallowed_extrapolations: Declare a heater inherently safer across scenarios
Infer rod/plate energy without their time-to-TR

solver_view.stem: A chamber is deemed airtight if it can sustain 20 atm for 10 min with leakage not exceeding 1 kPa/min. In a battery TR experiment performed in this chamber, constant heater power is 265 W and heating stops at TR onset. Which statement(s) are warranted solely from the airtightness criterion?
options: A Meeting the criterion bounds leakage rate during the TR event to ≤1 kPa/min.
B Meeting the criterion supports that pre-test leakage is small relative to a rapid TR pressure rise.
C Meeting the criterion guarantees no oxygen enters during the entire experiment.
D Meeting the criterion is sufficient to claim all measured gases originate from the battery.
E Meeting the criterion implies internal pressure never exceeds 20 atm during TR.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Criterion is a pre-test check at 20 atm for 10 min, not a bound during dynamic TR.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: It indicates the chamber can be made sufficiently tight in a static check; suggests leakage is controlled at least pre-test.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Airtightness check does not guarantee zero ingress over all times/pressures/temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Even with tightness, residual gases, outgassing, or contamination cannot be excluded from criterion alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Sustaining 20 atm in a leak test does not cap maximum pressure reached during an experiment.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interpret qualification criteria without overextending
analysis.primary_boundary: Static leak test ≠ dynamic TR guarantees
disallowed_extrapolations: Claim no ingress/egress during TR from the criterion
Claim measured gas is purely battery-origin

solver_view.stem: Three heater techniques (rod, coil, plate) are used to trigger TR in the same high-energy-density NCM811 46950 cell. In all tests, heater power is held constant at 265 W and heating stops at TR onset. Coil case: TR occurs after 225 s at 140.07 °C, which is higher than rod and plate. Which explanation(s) are consistent with these facts without adding new data?
options: A Heater technique can change spatial heating nonuniformity, shifting when/where the first runaway spot forms.
B The coil must transfer heat more efficiently, so the cell reaches higher temperature before runaway.
C Rod and plate likely create different temperature gradients, so a lower measured temperature can still coincide with hotter internal spots.
D Because power is constant, the total heat absorbed by the cell is identical at TR onset for all three heaters.
E The higher coil onset temperature proves the coil suppresses exothermic reactions until 140.07 °C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Different heating techniques at identical power can alter spatial heat distribution, plausibly changing initiation location and onset conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher onset temperature does not imply higher heat-transfer efficiency; it could reflect measurement location or hotspot formation differences.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Different heater geometries can yield different gradients; a lower measured temperature may still coexist with a localized hotter region.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Absorbed heat to onset depends on duration and losses; only coil time is given, so equality cannot be concluded.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Mechanistic suppression of reactions is not established; onset temperature alone cannot prove reaction kinetics were inhibited by the heater.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish consistent mechanisms from proof claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Onset temperature does not prove transfer or kinetics
intended_error_modes: mechanism proof from a single metric
disallowed_extrapolations: Infer heat-transfer coefficients from onset temperature
Infer reaction inhibition without reaction measurements

solver_view.prompt: A team wants to claim: “Using a coil heater makes the TR test safer because it triggers TR later and at a higher temperature.” Using only the following facts: (i) constant heater power 265 W in all tests; (ii) coil TR occurs after 225 s at 140.07 °C; (iii) coil onset temperature is higher than rod and plate; (iv) chamber airtightness is qualified by sustaining 20 atm for 10 min with leakage ≤1 kPa/min. Write a brief examiner response: what is supported, and what is not supported?

ground_truth.gold_answer: Supported: heater technique affects TR onset metrics; under identical 265 W power, the coil case reached TR at 225 s and 140.07 °C, higher than rod/plate. Not supported: a general “safer” conclusion, or claims about oxygen ingress, gas source purity, or energy-at-onset for rod/plate without their times and losses. Airtightness criterion only indicates a successful pre-test leak check at 20 atm, not dynamic TR sealing guarantees.
must_include: Leak test is static, not dynamic TR guarantee
disallowed_extrapolations: Claim coil reduces hazard in all scenarios
Assume zero oxygen ingress during TR
Compute rod/plate energy-to-TR without time data

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Write a boundary-limited evaluation statement
analysis.primary_boundary: From onset metrics to safety is unjustified

solver_view.stem: A pouch-cell venting hazard model uses a gas sensor to trigger a fire alarm. The alarm logic is: trigger when detected gas concentration is at least 5 ppm. The sensor’s stated detection criterion is also 5 ppm. Which statements are justified from this information alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The alarm threshold equals the sensor detection criterion.
B Any nonzero gas release will trigger the alarm.
C The alarm cannot trigger below 5 ppm by design.
D A 5 ppm reading implies a hazardous or flammable condition.
E The alarm timing is independent of airflow or mixing.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Both the detection criterion and alarm trigger threshold are explicitly 5 ppm.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Alarm triggers at ≥5 ppm, not for any nonzero concentration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Trigger rule is defined at ≥5 ppm; below that, trigger is not specified.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence links 5 ppm to flammability or hazard; it is a detection/trigger choice.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Alarm depends on measured concentration, which can vary with mixing/ventilation; independence is not supported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Threshold logic vs safety meaning
analysis.primary_boundary: Trigger threshold does not imply hazard
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring flammability or toxicity at 5 ppm
Assuming alarm always captures first release

solver_view.stem: A pouch-battery venting analysis reports “extensive CFD simulations” performed in ANSYS FLUENT 2020 R2. No further validation details are provided here. Which conclusions are defensible from this information alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The tool used was ANSYS FLUENT 2020 R2.
B The results must be accurate because a commercial CFD code was used.
C Mesh independence and model validation were necessarily performed.
D The simulations alone establish real-world alarm performance at 5 ppm.
E The statement is compatible with either 2D or 3D CFD.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The simulation code is explicitly named as ANSYS FLUENT 2020 R2.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Using commercial software does not guarantee correct physics, inputs, or numerical convergence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No information states mesh independence, uncertainty quantification, or experimental validation steps.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Simulations do not by themselves establish real sensor/alarm performance without sensor dynamics and deployment context.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The statement identifies software but not dimensionality; both 2D and 3D are possible.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Modeling claims vs proven performance
analysis.primary_boundary: Tool choice doesn’t prove validity
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming validation/convergence steps occurred
Equating simulation with field alarm reliability

solver_view.prompt: You are asked to sign off a claim: “This system provides early warning of venting because the alarm triggers at 5 ppm and venting is modeled with CFD.” Using only the following facts: (i) sensor detection criterion is 5 ppm, (ii) alarm triggers at ≥5 ppm, (iii) simulations used ANSYS FLUENT 2020 R2, state what you can and cannot conclude, and why.

ground_truth.gold_answer: You can conclude only that the alarm logic uses a 5 ppm threshold matching the stated detection criterion, and that CFD simulations were run in ANSYS FLUENT 2020 R2. You cannot conclude “early warning” or field effectiveness, because no information is given about sensor placement, response time, mixing/ventilation, or model validation against reality.
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming 5 ppm indicates hazard/flammability
Claiming CFD implies validated real-world performance
Assuming alarm timing independent of airflow/mixing

analysis.target_cognitive_object: What conclusions are not justified
analysis.primary_boundary: Threshold+CFD ≠ validated early warning

solver_view.stem: A 4-cell battery module is tested with different inter-cell thermal insulation materials (TIMs): mica plate, SiAG pre-oxidized fiber, aluminosilicate ceramic fiber, nano SiAG felt, and a no-insulation baseline. Outcomes reported include whether thermal runaway propagates to Cells 2–4 and whether all Cells 1–4 combust. Which statements are supported by the observations? (Select all that apply.)
options: A Propagation to Cells 2–4 was inhibited by mica plate, SiAG pre-oxidized fiber, and nano SiAG felt.
B Aluminosilicate ceramic fiber inhibited propagation to Cells 2–4 but still allowed Cell 1 to combust.
C No insulation and aluminosilicate ceramic fiber both led to combustion of all Cells 1–4.
D All tested TIMs reduced propagation compared with no insulation.
E Based on these results, mica plate will prevent propagation in any larger pack geometry.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly states the reported inhibited propagation materials.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Contradicts the reported failure of aluminosilicate ceramic fiber to prevent propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Matches the reported full-module combustion for both conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Overgeneralizes; one tested TIM failed similarly to no insulation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Unjustified scale-up claim; evidence is limited to the tested 4-cell module context.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish supported outcomes from overgeneralized safety claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Cannot scale module result to any larger pack
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing to different pack geometries or sizes
Assuming partial success for failed TIM
Claiming all TIMs outperform baseline

solver_view.stem: A temperature-mitigation metric is defined as ΔT_TIM n = T_(n,b max) − T_(n+1,f max), comparing the maximum back-surface temperature of Cell n to the maximum front-surface temperature of Cell n+1 during the event. For TIM1, reported values are: 578°C (Nano SiAG felt), 541°C (mica plate), 523°C (SiAG pre-oxidized fiber), −152°C (aluminosilicate ceramic fiber), −767°C (no insulation). Which statements are justified? (Select all that apply.)
options: A A negative ΔT_TIM1 means Cell 2 front-surface peak exceeded Cell 1 back-surface peak.
B Ranking TIMs by ΔT_TIM1 alone is sufficient to predict whether Cells 2–4 will combust.
C Nano SiAG felt had the largest positive ΔT_TIM1 among the listed cases.
D Aluminosilicate ceramic fiber had a higher ΔT_TIM1 than the no-insulation baseline.
E A larger ΔT_TIM1 necessarily implies lower toxic gas emissions (CO, NO2, SO2).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: From the definition, negative ΔT implies T_(n+1,f max) > T_(n,b max).

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: ΔT is one metric; propagation outcome is separately reported and not guaranteed by ΔT alone.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 578°C is the largest positive value in the provided list.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: −152°C is greater than −767°C, so aluminosilicate exceeds baseline by this metric.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No stated necessary link between ΔT magnitude and toxic gas emissions across TIMs.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interpret metric sign and resist single-metric determinism
analysis.primary_boundary: Metric does not prove combustion or gas outcomes
disallowed_extrapolations: Using ΔT alone to guarantee propagation outcome
Inferring toxic gas reduction from ΔT only
Inferring mechanisms beyond the defined temperatures

solver_view.stem: A study evaluates TIM performance using (i) temperature profiles via ΔT, (ii) toxic gas emissions (CO, NO2, SO2), and (iii) structural/thermal stability using SEM and TGA. Which statements about what can be concluded are defensible? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The evaluation considers both thermal metrics and toxic gas emissions, not temperature alone.
B SEM and TGA results, by themselves, determine whether thermal runaway propagation will occur in a module.
C From the listed metrics, one may assess TIM performance along multiple dimensions (thermal, emissions, stability).
D Because gases are measured, the study necessarily identifies the chemical reactions producing CO, NO2, SO2.
E Any TIM with better TGA stability must outperform all others in propagation inhibition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Metrics explicitly include ΔT and toxic gas emissions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEM/TGA are characterization tools; they do not alone determine module propagation outcomes.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The stated approach spans temperature, emissions, and stability assessments.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Measuring gas species does not necessarily identify their formation pathways without additional evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No stated monotonic link between TGA stability ranking and propagation inhibition ranking.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate measurement scope from mechanistic or deterministic claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Metrics do not uniquely determine mechanisms or outcomes
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring reaction mechanisms from species list alone
Claiming SEM/TGA alone predicts propagation
Assuming stability rank equals propagation rank

solver_view.prompt: You must brief a safety team on what can and cannot be concluded from a 4-cell module test comparing TIMs (mica plate, SiAG pre-oxidized fiber, aluminosilicate ceramic fiber, nano SiAG felt) and a no-insulation baseline. Summarize (i) the observed propagation/combustion outcomes and (ii) two boundary-limited statements about what these results do NOT justify (e.g., scale-up, causality, single-metric determinism). Keep it evidence-grounded.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Observed: mica plate, SiAG pre-oxidized fiber, and nano SiAG felt inhibited propagation to Cells 2–4; no insulation and aluminosilicate ceramic fiber led to combustion of all Cells 1–4. Not justified: claiming these results guarantee performance in other pack sizes/geometries or concluding toxic-gas reductions/chemical mechanisms from temperature metrics alone.
must_include: Which TIMs inhibited propagation to Cells 2–4
Which conditions led to combustion of all cells
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing performance in larger packs or different layouts
Inferring reaction pathways from measured gases
Using ΔT alone to guarantee propagation outcome
Claiming universal reduction of toxic gases

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Communicate supported findings with explicit inference boundaries
analysis.primary_boundary: Module outcomes do not generalize or prove mechanisms

solver_view.stem: Three identical LIBs are externally heated at three positions: side heating (SH), bottom heating (BH), and large-surface heating (LSH). All heaters deliver the same power density (1.03 W/cm2). Measured average internal thermal-runaway propagation velocities are: SH 2.035±0.058 mm/s, BH 1.942±0.217 mm/s, LSH 0.296±0.007 mm/s. Which statements are supported?
options: A SH propagates faster than BH on average under these test conditions.
B Equal heating power density implies equal total heating energy input for SH, BH, and LSH.
C LSH shows substantially slower internal propagation than SH and BH under these conditions.
D From these data, LSH is guaranteed safer than SH in any pack-level propagation scenario.
E The larger scatter in BH implies BH is always more sensitive to disturbances than SH.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Mean νTR is higher for SH than BH as reported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Equal power density does not fix heater area or duration; total energy may differ.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LSH νTR is far smaller than both SH and BH values.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cell-level internal velocity does not guarantee pack-level safety across layouts and conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: One dataset showing larger SD cannot support an 'always' sensitivity claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Differentiate supported comparisons from energy/scale inferences
analysis.primary_boundary: Power density and cell metrics do not imply pack safety
intended_error_modes: confusing power density with total energy
disallowed_extrapolations: Pack-level safety guarantee from νTR
Equal total energy from equal W/cm2

solver_view.stem: A lab defines thermal runaway (TR) as occurring only when ALL of these are met: Th temperature rise rate exceeds 1 °C/s, mass loss rate continues to exceed 0.5 g/s, and a considerable amount of white smoke is ejected from the safety valve. Which conclusions are justified about TR labeling under this definition?
options: A A case with Th rise rate >1 °C/s but no sustained mass loss >0.5 g/s is not TR by this definition.
B Any event with white smoke ejection alone must be labeled TR by this definition.
C This definition can classify a rapid self-heating event as non-TR if one required observable is absent.
D This definition proves the cell reached the same internal peak temperature in all TR cases.
E This definition is independent of sensor placement because it relies only on rates.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: TR requires all listed criteria; missing sustained mass loss fails the definition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: White smoke is necessary but not sufficient; other criteria are also required.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Even with fast heating, absent any required criterion, it is not TR by definition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The definition specifies rates and smoke, not internal peak temperature equivalence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Th is a particular measured temperature; placement can affect the measured rise rate.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Reason about definitional criteria and classification boundaries
analysis.primary_boundary: Definition does not imply mechanisms or peak temperatures
disallowed_extrapolations: Peak temperature equivalence from TR label
Sensor-independence claims from rate criteria

solver_view.stem: Three heating configurations use the same heater power density (1.03 W/cm2) but yield very different internal TR propagation velocities: SH ≈2.0 mm/s, BH ≈1.9 mm/s, LSH ≈0.30 mm/s. Which statements are defensible without assuming anything about heater area, heating duration, or cell construction beyond what is stated?
options: A Heating position can strongly affect internal propagation velocity even at equal heater power density.
B LSH must have received less total heat input than SH because its propagation was slower.
C SH and BH propagation velocities are close enough that they are necessarily identical within experimental uncertainty.
D Equal power density guarantees identical boundary temperature histories for all three configurations.
E The data alone identify the physical mechanism responsible for LSH being slower.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Despite equal W/cm2, νTR differs greatly among positions, showing strong configuration dependence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Slower propagation does not prove lower total heat input; area/time are unknown.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Close means not identical; uncertainties overlap status is not provided as a necessity claim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Same W/cm2 does not ensure identical thermal boundary conditions across geometries and contact.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Only outcome metrics are given; no mechanistic diagnostics are provided to identify causes.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate demonstrated configuration effects from mechanistic/energy assertions
analysis.primary_boundary: Outcome differences do not reveal mechanism or heat input
intended_error_modes: assuming identical boundary conditions from W/cm2
disallowed_extrapolations: Total heat input ranking from νTR alone
Mechanism conclusion without diagnostics

solver_view.prompt: You must report whether a test reached thermal runaway (TR) using only this lab criterion: TR occurs only when Th rise rate exceeds 1 °C/s, mass loss rate continues to exceed 0.5 g/s, AND considerable white smoke ejects from the safety valve. A test shows Th rise rate peaks at 1.2 °C/s briefly, white smoke is observed, but mass loss rate exceeds 0.5 g/s only momentarily and does not continue. What is the correct classification, and what key limitation should be stated when communicating the result?

ground_truth.gold_answer: Classify as non-TR under the stated criterion because the mass loss rate did not continue above 0.5 g/s, even though Th rise rate briefly exceeded 1 °C/s and white smoke occurred. State the limitation that this is a definition-based label tied to those observables and does not by itself establish internal peak temperature, mechanism, or pack-level hazard.
must_include: All three criteria must be met
Limitation: label doesn’t prove mechanism/peak temperature
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming internal peak temperature reached TR levels
Claiming pack-level safety or hazard from this label alone

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Apply a conjunctive TR definition and communicate boundaries
analysis.primary_boundary: Definition-based TR label is not mechanistic proof
intended_error_modes: treating any one indicator as sufficient

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery pack is operated with a design requirement: all cell temperatures must remain within an explicitly stated acceptable operating range during normal duty. A designer proposes adding a PCM layer (phase change temperature 58°C) wrapped with an aerogel felt layer (thermal conductivity 0.018 W m−1 K−1, density 200 kg m−3, porosity >90%). Which statements are justified from this information alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The acceptable operating range is −20°C to 60°C.
B Using a PCM with 58°C phase change necessarily guarantees cell temperature stays below 60°C.
C Aerogel felt is a low-thermal-conductivity material in this design context.
D Aerogel felt must reduce peak temperature for any duty cycle because insulation always improves safety.
E The PCM phase change temperature is below the upper limit of the stated acceptable range.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The acceptable operating temperature range is explicitly stated as −20°C to 60°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A phase change point alone cannot guarantee a maximum cell temperature; heat input and thermal resistances are unstated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The aerogel felt conductivity value given (0.018 W m−1 K−1) supports describing it as low conductivity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Safety/peak temperature improvement is not guaranteed; insulation can also hinder heat rejection depending on duty cycle.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 58°C is numerically below the upper acceptable limit of 60°C.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish supported specs from implied performance guarantees
analysis.primary_boundary: Material properties ≠ pack-level temperature guarantee
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing max temperature from PCM melting point
Claiming universal safety improvement from insulation

solver_view.stem: Two alternative insulation wraps are considered for the same battery pack: (i) an aerogel felt with thermal conductivity 0.018 W m−1 K−1, density 200 kg m−3, porosity >90%; (ii) an unspecified foam with unknown properties. Only the aerogel felt properties are known. Which conclusions are admissible without assuming any additional data? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The aerogel felt is highly porous (>90%).
B The aerogel felt must be lighter per unit volume than any foam, because high porosity always implies minimum density.
C The aerogel felt density is 200 kg m−3.
D The foam will provide worse insulation than the aerogel felt.
E The aerogel felt has thermal conductivity 0.018 W m−1 K−1.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Porosity of the aerogel felt is stated as more than 90%.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Porosity alone cannot order densities across materials; foam density is unknown and porosity-density relation isn’t provided.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Aerogel felt density is explicitly given as 200 kg m−3.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Foam insulation performance cannot be compared because its thermal conductivity and structure are unspecified.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Aerogel felt thermal conductivity is explicitly given as 0.018 W m−1 K−1.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Avoid comparative claims without comparable evidence
analysis.primary_boundary: Unknown comparator blocks performance ranking
disallowed_extrapolations: Ranking insulation with missing foam properties
Inferring density ordering from porosity alone

solver_view.stem: A pack-level thermal management concept uses a PCM (phase change temperature 58°C) in contact with cells, plus an outer aerogel felt layer (thermal conductivity 0.018 W m−1 K−1). The pack must operate within an acceptable cell temperature window of −20°C to 60°C. Which statement is NOT justified from the given information? (Select one.)
options: A The acceptable window spans 80°C in width.
B The PCM phase change temperature lies within the acceptable operating window.
C The aerogel felt thermal conductivity is 0.018 W m−1 K−1.
D The design will necessarily keep all cells within the acceptable window under all ambient conditions.
E The PCM phase change temperature is close to the upper bound of the acceptable window.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: From −20°C to 60°C, the span is 80°C by subtraction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 58°C is between −20°C and 60°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Thermal conductivity is directly provided as 0.018 W m−1 K−1.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A universal guarantee across all ambients/duty cycles is not supported; many required conditions are unspecified.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 58°C is numerically near the 60°C upper bound relative to the full window.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Reject unjustified system-level guarantees
analysis.primary_boundary: Material temperatures don’t imply compliance for all conditions
disallowed_extrapolations: All-ambient, all-duty-cycle compliance guarantee

solver_view.prompt: You are asked to sign off a claim: "Adding a 58°C PCM plus aerogel felt (k = 0.018 W m−1 K−1, density 200 kg m−3, porosity >90%) ensures the battery pack always stays within its acceptable operating temperatures." Using only the stated acceptable temperature window and the listed material properties, write a conservative technical response: what you can affirm, and what you cannot conclude.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Affirm only the stated specifications: the acceptable operating window is −20°C to 60°C; the PCM phase change temperature is 58°C; the aerogel felt has k = 0.018 W m−1 K−1, density 200 kg m−3, and porosity >90%. You cannot conclude the design will always keep cells within −20°C to 60°C, because pack heat generation, boundary conditions, geometry, and heat rejection are not given.
must_include: Cannot guarantee pack-level compliance without duty/ambient details
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming universal temperature compliance
Claiming safety improvement under all regimes

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Compose a defensible, bounded engineering sign-off statement
analysis.primary_boundary: Specs are not performance proof

solver_view.stem: A monitoring system uses a switching strategy: below a temperature threshold it runs an LSTM to predict battery temperature; once the detected battery temperature exceeds the threshold it captures a thermal image and runs an MMTPFNN to predict thermal runaway (TR) propagation. The threshold is 60 °C. Select all statements that are directly justified by this setup.
options: A The model-selection decision boundary is 60 °C.
B The system guarantees it will detect TR onset before any cell exceeds 60 °C.
C When detected temperature is below 60 °C, the system runs LSTM to predict battery temperature.
D Once detected temperature exceeds 60 °C, the system captures a thermal image and runs MMTPFNN for TR propagation prediction.
E Switching at 60 °C is optimal for all battery chemistries and pack designs.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The switching strategy explicitly uses a 60 °C temperature threshold.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No performance guarantee about TR onset detection is stated; thresholding alone doesn’t ensure early detection.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Below 60 °C, LSTM is specified to predict the battery temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Above 60 °C, the system captures thermal images and runs MMTPFNN to predict TR propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence supports universal optimality of the 60 °C threshold across designs or chemistries.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish stated logic from implied performance guarantees
analysis.primary_boundary: Switching rule does not imply detection optimality
disallowed_extrapolations: Universal safety/optimality claims from a threshold rule
TR onset timing guarantees not specified in stem

solver_view.stem: A test fixture applies external heating to a battery using a constant heating power. The heating power is calculated from Pheat = (−15.41Ecell + 4.72mcell), and the calculated heating power is 60 W. Select all conclusions that are warranted without knowing Ecell or mcell values.
options: A The applied heating power is 60 W.
B Increasing Ecell necessarily increases the applied heating power.
C Increasing mcell necessarily increases the applied heating power.
D The equation implies heating power is independent of both Ecell and mcell.
E The equation alone proves heating power can never be negative.

option_judgments.label: Correct

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Reason about parameter sensitivity and non-implied constraints

solver_view.prompt: A safety engineer claims: “Because the system switches to thermal imaging and an MMTPFNN predictor once detected temperature exceeds 60 °C, it can be relied upon as a general early-warning system for thermal runaway in any battery pack.” Provide a technically grounded rebuttal limited to what is justified by the described switching logic. Do not introduce external datasets or unstated performance metrics.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The description only specifies a 60 °C switching rule: below 60 °C it runs LSTM temperature prediction; above 60 °C it captures a thermal image and runs MMTPFNN to predict TR propagation. This alone does not justify early-warning performance, detection timing relative to TR onset, or generality across battery packs/chemistries.

No stated guarantee of early warning or universality

Claiming optimality/universality across designs
Assuming accuracy/latency without data

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Challenge unjustified system-level safety claims from control logic
analysis.primary_boundary: Switching strategy ≠ validated early-warning capability

solver_view.stem: A lab evaluates a sandwich barrier for suppressing pouch-cell thermal-runaway propagation. The pouch cell is NCM, 10,000 mAh, 3.7 V nominal (3.0 V cut-off, 4.2 V max). The sandwich uses FRPCM and AEGF layers, each 1–3 mm thick, total 3–8 mm. AEGF thermal conductivity is 0.018 W/m·K at 25 °C. Which statements are justified from this information alone?
options: A The test cell format/chemistry is an NCM pouch cell of 10,000 mAh.
B The sandwich will necessarily reduce TR propagation because AEGF has very low thermal conductivity.
C The overall sandwich thickness is within 3–8 mm, using 1–3 mm FRPCM and 1–3 mm AEGF layers.
D Increasing sandwich thickness from 3 to 8 mm must monotonically improve suppression performance.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cell is explicitly specified as NCM pouch, 10,000 mAh.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

option_judgments.label: Correct

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissible conclusions vs performance overreach
analysis.primary_boundary: Property/spec range does not imply effectiveness
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring TRP suppression effectiveness from conductivity alone
Assuming monotonic improvement with thickness
Using 25°C property to model TR heat flux

solver_view.stem: A flame-retardant PCM (FRPCM) is prepared for a battery safety experiment. Its mass ratios are 63% PA, 7% EG, 3% kaolin, and 27% IFR. Which statements are justified from these details alone?

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: EG is only 7%, not dominant compared with PA at 63%.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

option_judgments.label: Correct

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

solver_view.stem: Two cells are described only by their nameplate information: both are NCM pouch cells, 10,000 mAh, nominal 3.7 V, discharge cut-off 3.0 V, maximum charge 4.2 V. An engineer claims these values are sufficient to quantify stored electrical energy for safety ranking and to infer thermal runaway severity. Which statement is the best engineering judgment?
options: A Nameplate voltage limits alone are enough to infer thermal runaway severity ranking.
B Capacity and nominal voltage allow only a rough electrical energy estimate; severity cannot be inferred without additional abuse/thermal data.

E Because chemistry is NCM, all cells will show the same propagation behavior regardless of packaging or environment.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Voltage limits don’t encode abuse conditions, heat transfer, or reaction kinetics.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Nameplate values support only rough energy; severity needs more than electrical ratings.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cut-off voltage is a control limit, not a guarantee against runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Same max voltage doesn’t imply same resistance or fault heating behavior.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Packaging/environment effects are not constrained by chemistry nameplate alone.

disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring TR severity from voltage limits alone

Assuming identical propagation from chemistry label

solver_view.prompt: You must write a closed-book test plan paragraph using only the following fixed facts: NCM pouch cell, 10,000 mAh; nominal 3.7 V (3.0 V cut-off, 4.2 V max); FRPCM composition 63% PA/7% EG/3% kaolin/27% IFR; sandwich uses FRPCM and AEGF layers (each 1–3 mm), total 3–8 mm; AEGF thermal conductivity 0.018 W/m·K at 25 °C. State (i) two things you can claim up front, and (ii) three critical unknowns you must NOT claim without further measurements, focusing on TR propagation suppression.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Can claim: the cell is an NCM pouch cell rated 10,000 mAh and 3.7 V nominal with 3.0/4.2 V limits; the sandwich geometry and FRPCM composition and AEGF 25°C conductivity are defined within stated ranges. Must not claim without measurements: that the sandwich reduces or monotonically improves TR propagation; that 25°C conductivity predicts high-temperature TR heat transfer; that composition implies mechanical integrity, venting tolerance, or overall severity reduction.

disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing suppression/severity outcomes

Extrapolating 25°C material property to TR temperatures

solver_view.stem: A reduced-order thermal runaway network (TRN) model is created by redistributing heat-source terms and correcting the trigger criterion. In validation against a baseline model, the TRN predicts thermal runaway trigger time with error within 1 min. Which statements are justified from this information alone?

C The reported “within 1 min” refers specifically to trigger-time accuracy, not necessarily peak temperature accuracy.
D Because it is reduced-order, the TRN must run faster than any traditional thermal network model.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Directly restates the model changes described.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Claims universal validity beyond stated comparison context.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

solver_view.stem: A module’s reported thermal runaway propagation interval decreases by 72% (from 611 s to 176 s). A note states this indicates controlling initial thermal spread within the module is more critical than mitigating thermal propagation between modules. Which statements are supported?

solver_view.stem: During thermal runaway propagation, more than 70% of the total energy is dissipated via liquid cooling plates, exceeding heat transfer through neighboring battery surfaces and the tab connector. Which statements follow directly?

solver_view.stem: A lab reports tests on commercial 21700 NMC cells (5 Ah, 3.7 V nominal) inside a 12 m3 chamber. option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Capability range is not evidence of tested outcomes; altitude safety extrapolation is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

option_judgments.label: Incorrect

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Volume does not guarantee oxygen sufficiency; consumption depends on event severity and duration, not provided.

Non-conclusions: do not claim behavior at 15 kPa or any untested pressure; do not claim safety, linear/monotonic pressure trends, or dominance of pressure over other variables from setup alone.

solver_view.stem: A cylindrical cell is treated as an isothermal heat source at 500 K. It is surrounded by two concentric PCM layers: inner PCM1 = RT82 (diameter 30 mm) and outer PCM2 = RT27 (diameter 42 mm). The outer wall of PCM2 is held at 300 K. Select all statements that are justified by this setup alone, without adding new assumptions about material properties or time dependence.
options: A The configuration imposes an inward-to-outward temperature driving force from the cell toward the 300 K outer wall.
B The RT27 outer layer must melt before the RT82 inner layer because it is closer to the 300 K boundary.
C The RT82/RT27 assignment is trans-radial: RT82 is closest to the cell and RT27 is outermost.
D The 42 mm diameter guarantees a lower peak cell temperature than any smaller outer diameter for all PCMs.
E The system must reach steady state since both boundaries are fixed temperatures.
F A unique melting sequence can be concluded (which PCM melts first) without any thermal property data.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Boundary temperatures are 500 K at the cell and 300 K at the outer wall, creating outward heat-transfer driving force.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Melting order depends on melting points and heat transfer; proximity to 300 K alone cannot determine melt-first.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: PCM closest to the cell is RT82 and outermost PCM is RT27 in the stated arrangement.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Peak cell temperature is fixed at 500 K by the model; diameter change cannot lower it under this boundary condition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Fixed boundary temperatures do not guarantee steady state; transient evolution and phase change may persist.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Without thermal properties and melting points/latent heats, melting sequence is underdetermined.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate boundary conditions from melt-sequence claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Melting order not implied by geometry alone
intended_error_modes: Assuming monotonic melt order from boundary proximity
Generalizing geometric size to universal cooling benefit
disallowed_extrapolations: Any conclusion requiring PCM thermal properties or melting points

solver_view.stem: In the same trans-radial configuration, adding 6% SWCNT nanoparticles to RT82 (PCM1) reportedly increases its melting temperature distribution by up to 15.27% relative to pure RT82. Select all conclusions that are warranted from that statement alone.
options: A Relative to pure RT82, the reported metric for RT82+6% SWCNT is higher by up to 15.27%.
B The modification necessarily improves overall battery thermal safety under all operating scenarios.
C The reported change alone is insufficient to infer faster or slower melting without additional transport/latent-heat information.
D The modification proves RT82+6% SWCNT has higher thermal conductivity than pure RT82.
E The reported percentage change can be applied unchanged to RT27 in the outer layer.
F The reported change implies the cell temperature will drop below 500 K in this model.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: It directly restates the reported observation: up to 15.27% increase versus pure RT82 in that metric.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A single metric shift does not justify a system-level safety guarantee across scenarios.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Melting dynamics require heat transfer and latent heat; a distribution increase alone does not determine melt rate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No conductivity data are given; melting temperature distribution change does not prove conductivity increase.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The statement concerns RT82 with SWCNT; transferring the same percent to RT27 is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The cell is specified as a 500 K heat source; this statement does not change that boundary condition.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Avoid inferring mechanism/safety from a single reported metric
analysis.primary_boundary: Metric change ≠ conductivity, melt rate, or safety
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying RT82 result to RT27 or to all scenarios

solver_view.prompt: You are asked to justify the claim: “In the trans-radial RT82/RT27 design (RT82 inner, RT27 outer), the outer RT27 layer will melt first because it is adjacent to the 300 K boundary.” Using only the stated setup (cell at 500 K, outer wall at 300 K, RT82 inner/RT27 outer, given diameters, and the reported SWCNT effect on RT82), write a brief examiner-style critique identifying what can be concluded and what cannot.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The setup fixes boundary temperatures (500 K at the cell, 300 K at the outer wall) and the layer ordering (RT82 inside, RT27 outside) with stated diameters. From this alone, you can assert an outward heat-transfer driving force, but you cannot deduce which PCM melts first. Melting sequence depends on melting points, latent heats, and heat-transfer properties, none of which are provided. The reported “up to 15.27%” change applies only to RT82 with 6% SWCNT and does not determine RT27 melting or the melt order.
must_include: Outward driving force from 500 K to 300 K
RT82 is inner; RT27 is outer
SWCNT result applies to RT82 only
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming a specific PCM melts first
Claiming improved safety or conductivity without data
Applying RT82 SWCNT effect to RT27

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Critique an underdetermined causal claim

intended_error_modes: Treating one metric shift as mechanism proof

solver_view.stem: A 46950 NCM811 cell is tested at 100% SOC using three external heating techniques: rod, coil, and plate. option_judgments.label: Incorrect

solver_view.stem: A lab defines thermal runaway (TR) as occurring when the measured heat generation rate exceeds 1 °C·min−1. B If heat mode is active, TR cannot occur in that test.
C Heat mode activation implies the system is operating below 0.05 °C·min−1 at that moment.
D Any test with average heat generation rate below 0.05 °C·min−1 is guaranteed safe from TR.
E Because 1 is much larger than 0.05, the ARC heat mode definition is stricter than the TR definition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Direct restatement of the TR definition threshold.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Activation criterion doesn’t preclude later rate increase above TR threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Heat mode activation condition explicitly references rates below 0.05 °C·min−1.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Average rate doesn’t rule out transient excursions above 1 °C·min−1.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: They are different conditions for different purposes; “stricter” isn’t implied.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate definitions from safety conclusions
analysis.primary_boundary: Activation criteria don’t imply prevention
disallowed_extrapolations: guaranteeing safety from a low-rate condition

solver_view.stem: A continuous-wave disk laser (maximum output 2000 W) irradiates a battery. The laser is set to 200 W with repeated irradiation: 0.4 s on, at 1.0 s intervals, stated to be equivalent to an average laser power of 57.1 W. Which statements are supported? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The instantaneous laser power during each pulse is 200 W.
B The time-averaged laser power is 57.1 W under the stated timing.
C The average laser power must be 80 W because duty cycle is 0.4/1.0.
D Because the laser’s maximum is 2000 W, the battery experienced 2000 W peaks.
E The average laser power alone determines whether thermal runaway occurs.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Power was set to 200 W during repeated irradiation periods.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Average power 57.1 W is explicitly stated as equivalent.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Contradicts the explicitly stated equivalent average power value.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Maximum capability does not imply it was used in this procedure.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TR depends on heat generation rate threshold; laser average power alone is insufficient.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish equipment rating, setpoint, and outcome
analysis.primary_boundary: Input power ≠ internal heat rate
disallowed_extrapolations: inferring TR from laser average power alone

solver_view.stem: A team reports two operational criteria: (i) thermal runaway occurs when heat generation rate exceeds 1 °C·min−1; (ii) an ARC “heat mode” is activated for heat generation rates below 0.05 °C·min−1. Which inferences are NOT justified from these statements alone? (Select all that apply.)
options: A The numerical thresholds 0.05 and 1 °C·min−1 refer to different operating regimes.
B A measured rate of 0.04 °C·min−1 is below the TR definition threshold.
C Once heat mode activates, the test will never reach thermal runaway.
D A measured rate of 1.1 °C·min−1 meets the stated TR definition.
E The ARC heat mode threshold is a validated early-warning indicator for imminent TR across cell types.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: This is justified: two distinct thresholds imply different regimes by definition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: This is justified: 0.04 is below 1 °C·min−1 TR threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Activation condition provides no guarantee the rate won’t later exceed 1 °C·min−1.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: This is justified: 1.1 exceeds the stated TR threshold definition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: No evidence of validation, prediction, or generalization across chemistries/cell types is provided.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identify non-implications from thresholds
analysis.primary_boundary: Cross-context validation not provided
disallowed_extrapolations: claiming universal early-warning validity

solver_view.prompt: You are reviewing a safety report that uses two statements: thermal runaway is defined when heat generation rate exceeds 1 °C·min−1, and an ARC “heat mode” activates when heat generation rates are below 0.05 °C·min−1. Write a brief critique identifying two conclusions the report must NOT claim without additional data, and state what can be concluded safely.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Do not claim that heat mode activation prevents or rules out later thermal runaway, or that the 0.05 °C·min−1 threshold predicts imminent TR (especially across cell types). Safely conclude only the stated operational definitions: TR corresponds to >1 °C·min−1 heat generation rate, and heat mode activates when the rate is <0.05 °C·min−1 at that time.
disallowed_extrapolations: Guaranteeing safety based on heat mode activation
Claiming universal early-warning capability

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Boundary critique of definition vs prediction
analysis.primary_boundary: Control state ≠ hazard state

solver_view.stem: A pouch lithium-ion capacitor is discharged with transient high rates spanning 1C–550C. Observations: (i) end-of-discharge temperature rise versus C-rate shows an upward parabolic trend with a maximum end temperature of 33.68°C at 200C; (ii) at 550C the end-of-discharge temperature decreases due to reduced discharge time; (iii) at 550C irreversible heat is ~80% of total heat. Which statements are supported?
options: A The end-of-discharge temperature versus C-rate is non-monotonic, peaking near 200C under these conditions.
B Because irreversible heat fraction is ~80% at 550C, the end-of-discharge temperature must exceed the 200C case.
C A shorter discharge duration can reduce end-of-discharge temperature even if the process is more irreversible-dominated.
D The observed 200C temperature maximum proves irreversible heat fraction is maximal at 200C.
E The data justify concluding 550C operation is thermally safer than 200C for all duty cycles.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Parabolic end-of-discharge temperature trend with a maximum at 200C is explicitly stated.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: End temperature at 550C is stated to decrease due to reduced discharge time; no “must exceed” inference is allowed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Stem supports reduced discharge time lowering end temperature, while irreversibility fraction can still be high.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No evidence links the 200C temperature maximum to a maximum in irreversible heat fraction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Claims are specific to end-of-discharge temperature in this setup; cannot generalize safety across all duty cycles.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Separate irreversibility fraction from temperature outcome
analysis.primary_boundary: No safety generalization across duty cycles
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring higher C-rate always means higher end temperature
Concluding overall safety from end temperature only
Inferring irreversible fraction trend from a temperature peak

solver_view.stem: In a thermal prediction exercise for the same pouch lithium-ion capacitor under transient high-rate discharge, several calculation methods are compared against measured temperatures. The intermittent current method matches measured temperature trends better than other methods because polarization internal resistance is significantly related to the discharge time interval. Which conclusions are justified?
options: A A method that accounts for the discharge time interval can better capture temperature trends when polarization resistance depends on that interval.
B The intermittent current method will remain most accurate for any cell format, chemistry, and current waveform.
C Better trend agreement alone proves the intermittent current method is physically correct in all internal heat-generation partitions.
D If polarization resistance is interval-dependent, ignoring interval structure can distort predicted temperature trends.
E The statement implies polarization resistance is independent of C-rate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Better agreement is attributed to polarization resistance being significantly related to discharge time interval.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence is limited to this context; cannot claim universal accuracy across formats, chemistries, and waveforms.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Trend agreement does not prove correctness of all internal heat partitions; only comparative trend matching is stated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: If polarization resistance depends on time interval, omitting interval effects can mis-predict temperature trends.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: No claim is made about C-rate dependence or independence; only interval dependence is stated.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguish fit-to-data from universal validity
analysis.primary_boundary: No generalization beyond tested context
disallowed_extrapolations: Universalizing a best method to all systems
Equating better trend match with full physical truth
Inferring C-rate independence from interval dependence

solver_view.prompt: You observe two seemingly conflicting facts during transient high-rate discharge of a pouch lithium-ion capacitor: (1) the end-of-discharge temperature versus C-rate peaks at 200C and then drops by 550C; (2) at 550C, irreversible heat is ~80% of total heat. Provide a defensible engineering explanation that reconciles these without introducing new measurements. Also state one key conclusion you must NOT draw from these facts.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The end-of-discharge temperature can drop at 550C because the discharge time is reduced, limiting time for temperature to rise, even though the heat generation is largely irreversible (~80%). A key non-conclusion is that 550C is generally “safer” or lower-heat overall than 200C across other duty cycles or conditions.
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding total heat at 550C is lower than at 200C
Concluding universal safety ranking across duty cycles
Inferring irreversible fraction versus C-rate trend

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Reconcile counterintuitive trends with time-scale reasoning
analysis.primary_boundary: Avoid safety or total-heat inference
